evidence: bundle.json: "planPath": "<plan-path>`". The operator confirms it in-session: the harvester's _plan_path grabs the first /ultrapowers it sees, which in any session containing the skill's own prose (like this one) is the <plan-path> placeholder. slash_ultrapowers count was 45 — almost all documentation/prose mentions, not the one real invocation (ARGUMENTS: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-25-ultralearn-feedback-loop.md).
implication: harvest_runs.py _plan_path must not take the first '/ultrapowers ' match; it should anchor on the actual invocation (the user turn carrying ARGUMENTS:/the slash command that launched the run, or the planPath that co-occurs with the Workflow tool_use), and explicitly skip matches inside fenced/quoted skill-prose. Add a fixture session that embeds the skill's own SKILL.md text to pin the regression.
title: Harvester planPath extracted the literal template string '<plan-path>'

evidence: bundle.json transcriptDir was a broken quoted fragment, and audit.note 'no agent-*.jsonl under ...', with audit.totals {turns:0, outputTokens:0} and agents []. The real Transcript dir lines were truncated artifacts ('Transcript dir:\') — the harvester latched onto a broken/quoted fragment instead of the actual per-run subagents/workflows/wf_... path that DID appear later (e.g. wf_3e23d73a-e46). The operator ran audit_run.py manually at the gate and got a full 14-row effort table, proving the data exists; the harvester simply pointed at the wrong path.
implication: The transcriptDir parser is fooled by documentation 'Transcript dir:\' fragments and stops before the real launch-result path. It should extract from the Workflow tool_result block (the authoritative 'Transcript dir: /Users/.../subagents/workflows/wf_*' line), not from prose, and validate the path exists before recording it. Until fixed, every harvested run that discusses ultrapowers loses its entire cost/effort signal.
title: transcriptDir mis-extracted to garbage, zeroing the entire audit block

evidence: bundle.json: gateReport null. Yet the transcript contains a full pre-merge gate: a per-task verdict table, integration HEAD verification (533a1df, then c2933449), a REJECT-then-Salvage-then-APPROVE arc, and an effort audit. The gate_report signature counter was 1 in the session scan. The harvester failed to parse/attach the report object even though the run clearly produced gate material.
implication: gateReport extraction likely keys on a report-format.md JSON object emitted by the engine, but this run's report surfaced as the orchestrator's rendered markdown tables rather than a single JSON blob — or the extractor depends on transcriptDir which was already broken. Harden gateReport detection to recover from the rendered report and decouple it from the transcriptDir failure so one mis-extraction doesn't cascade.
title: gateReport null despite a real gate exchange with a rich structured report

evidence: The suite was green (359 passed) but the integration review and completeness critic both said REJECT — and they were right. Task 1 (reader rubric, haiku) failed with an agent-error ('subagent completed without calling StructuredOutput'), which cascade-blocked Task 5. So only Tasks 2, 3, 4 merged; the green suite is a false signal because the tests that would catch the missing tasks are themselves missing. Tree sanity confirmed reading-lenses.md (T1) and SKILL.md (T5) absent on the integration branch.
implication: This is the verification-first thesis working (the completeness critic caught what tests.passed missed) — but it is also a recurring structural hazard worth a ledger cluster: when the failed/blocked tasks are the ones that ADD the tests, a green suite is meaningless. Consider a gate heuristic that cross-checks deliverable presence (plan's Create: paths) against the integration tree whenever any task is failed/cascade-blocked, so 'green + missing deliverables' is flagged loudly rather than relying solely on the critic.
title: First run merged 3/5 with a GREEN suite that was a false pass

evidence: Effort audit: impl:1 on claude-haiku-4-5 = 42 turns / 3148 output tokens (highest turn count of any agent, yet near-lowest output). Operator: the haiku tier implementer couldn't satisfy the engine's StructuredOutput contract even after a nudge. That's a tier-floor finding. The fix is mechanical: re-run Task 1 at sonnet, which unblocks Task 5. Salvage at sonnet succeeded clean on the first try.
implication: The cheap (haiku) tier cannot reliably satisfy the implementer structured-return contract; a single nudge then a hard task failure is a poor failure mode that cost a whole salvage cycle. Highest-leverage engine fix: auto-escalate tier on a StructuredOutput-compliance agent-error (retry at sonnet) before failing the task, or impose a haiku floor for structured implementer roles. The operator independently ranked this the #1 finding.
title: haiku implementer thrashed 42 turns, failed the StructuredOutput contract, cascade-blocking a downstream task

evidence: Fit analysis: 5 implementation tasks, widest wave 3, Acceptance suite — but the work builds a cross-project privacy guard writing into a public repo, a genuine data-integrity surface. Both the risk override and the width+T>=4 branch point the same way. Recommended Ultrapowers (risk: cross-project privacy leak). Waves compiled cleanly; parallel width was real (wave 1 ran 3 independent tasks). The routing decision was sound and the wave shape held.
implication: Routing was correct and the recommender's two-branch convergence is reassuring. Minor note for the ledger: the 'risk override' here was the redaction guard's data-integrity surface (Task 4), which is exactly the kind of self-justifying privacy concern the ultralearn design itself is about — a clean example that the risk branch fires on real stakes, not reflex. No fix needed; record as a positive routing exemplar.
title: Routing recommendation matched the run, but the cited justification was the weaker of two valid reasons

evidence: The waves came out [[2,4],[1,3],[5]] not [[1,2,4],[3],[5]] — because Task 1's Produces line backticks merge_ledger.py (which Task 4 creates), so the compiler inferred a spurious prose-reference edge 4->1. That's exactly the hazard ultraplan's self-review warns about. compile_plan.py marker_conflicts surfaced it: 'prose-reference edge inferred: task prose references skills/ultralearn/scripts/merge_ledger.py created by Task 4.' Fixed by rewording before launch.
implication: The compiler infers real DAG edges from descriptive prose in interface/Produces fields, so naming a sibling's file in a description silently serializes parallel work. The compiler flagged it (good), but the operator — author of ultraplan — still walked into it, proving the authoring guidance isn't load-bearing at write time. Promote 'describe siblings by role, not by filename, in Produces/Consumes descriptions' into ultraplan's hard authoring rules, and give the compiler a distinct 'edge inferred from a description field — likely not a real dependency' warning class.
title: A 'Produces:' description backtick injected a phantom prose-reference edge that reshaped the waves

evidence: marker_conflicts: 'Test fixture:'/'Test fixtures:' lines unrecognized (only Create:/Modify:/Test: parsed), the gate task's '- None' bullet unrecognized, and '.gitignore' on a Modify: line filtered as a 'dotted attribute' ('non-path token(s) ignored (.gitignore) — Files entries list paths, not identifiers'). Operator: my Test fixture:/Test fixtures: lines and the gate's - None bullet aren't parsed, and Task 1's backtick of merge_ledger.py fabricated a 4->1 edge.
implication: The Files-line label set is too closed and the prose-oriented dotted-attribute filter misfires on legitimate dotfile paths. Accept Fixture(s):/Test fixture(s): (as writes) and leading-dot path tokens on Files lines, and let gate tasks declare no files without a near-miss warning. Cheap, low-risk, one PR; removes recurring false noise at the wave-plan gate.
title: compile_plan.py Files-label parser dropped fixture paths and a dotfile from overlap inference

evidence: Operator answered design questions tersely via system-injected answers ('Ledger -> human-gated proposals', 'Merge to main + release + push') and said 'You pre-authorized me to choose, so I'm selecting Ultrapowers and launching now.' The human spent attention on planning (design Q&A, spec/plan approval) and at the gate (the Approve/Salvage/Redirect decision), not on implementation — the attention-shifting goal in CLAUDE.md realized.
implication: Positive confirmation that the planning-heavy / implementation-light attention split lands with a sophisticated operator. The terse system-injected answers suggest the operator is comfortable delegating; worth noting the design's autonomy posture matched operator trust here. No change needed.
title: Operator pre-authorized autonomous execution and stayed at the gate, exactly as the design intends

evidence: [Request interrupted by user] after the shipped close-out. The agent then reasoned: neither a heavyweight implementation plan nor an immediate ultralearn run is the right tool for 'analyze the six findings' — analyzing the findings is just reasoning over data already held, exactly what ultralearn distill will eventually do, doable by hand right now. The operator's interruption surfaced a tool-selection misjudgment (over-reaching toward planning machinery for a reasoning task).
implication: The operator's interrupt corrected an instinct to escalate to plan-building when manual distillation was the right altitude. Signal for the ultralearn SKILL: 'distill' should be runnable as a lightweight in-session reasoning pass over already-held findings, not framed as requiring a full harvest+read pipeline or a plan. Document the manual-distill affordance.
title: Operator interrupted the close-out to redirect from 'build a plan' toward direct reasoning over findings

evidence: Operator: I'm not writing these into docs/superpowers/observations/ledger.jsonl yet — on purpose. If I hand-seed the ledger, then next session's automated harvest+read 're-deriving' them proves nothing. Keeping them in memory only preserves the dogfood as a clean independent validation: the readers either surface these on their own, or they don't. They stay the answer key, not the input.
implication: The operator designed an honest acceptance test for ultralearn: the hand-found findings are a held-out answer key the readers must independently re-derive. This is a strong validation methodology that the ultralearn design should formalize — a way to run readers blind to a known answer key and score recall. Worth capturing in the ultralearn references as the canonical self-validation procedure.
title: Operator deliberately withheld the seed findings from the ledger to keep dogfooding a clean test

evidence: bundle.json audit.totals {turns:0, outputTokens:0}, agents [], because transcriptDir was mis-extracted. Yet the real effort table (run manually at the gate) shows 14 agents, e.g. impl:1 haiku 42t/3148tok, impl:3 opus 39t/10128tok, review:3 opus 29t/10502tok — substantial measurable effort the harvested record discards entirely.
implication: Every cost-lens conclusion for this run is unavailable from the bundle alone — the absence is a direct downstream consequence of the transcriptDir bug, not of a low-effort run. Until harvest_runs.py reliably resolves transcriptDir, the ledger's cost dimension is systematically empty for self-referential/doc-heavy sessions. Fixing transcriptDir extraction restores the entire cost lens; treat it as a prerequisite, not a separate feature.
title: Audit metrics are zeroed in the bundle — the cost lens is blind for this run

evidence: Effort audit printed 'No tier-misrank candidates' even though impl:1 (haiku) ran 42 turns producing only 3148 tokens and then FAILED — the worst-behaved agent in the run. Operator: the heuristic is purely relative (needs >=2 same-model peers); blind to absolute/single-sample outliers and errored agents. Also '2 agent file(s) unclassified — baked-prompt phrases may have drifted.'
implication: The misrank detector only fires on relative comparison within >=2 same-model peers, so a lone thrashing or errored implementer is invisible. Add an absolute thrash signal (high turns / low output ratio) and surface agent-errored tasks explicitly. Since ultralearn reuses audit()'s extracted function, this directly improves the harvested cost lens for every future run.
title: audit_run.py misrank heuristic is blind to single-sample thrash and errored agents

evidence: Wave 1 ran 3 independent tasks ([1,2,4]) in parallel; tasks 2/3/4 merged clean (3 and 4 adversarial). But Task 1's haiku failure forced a second full Workflow launch (wf_f4a197ee-87b) re-running Task 1 at sonnet -> Task 5, roughly doubling the orchestration overhead for two tasks. Net suite grew 342->359->364 passed across the arc.
implication: The salvage path worked deterministically (resume:true, same integrationBranch, tasks 2/3/4 stayed put) — good. But the cost of the salvage was entirely attributable to the haiku tier-floor failure; an auto-escalate-on-contract-error engine fix would have made the first run succeed outright and eliminated the second launch. Reinforces the haiku tier-floor leverage from the cost angle.
title: Parallelism paid off on wave 1 but the run still required a full salvage cycle

evidence: The tool's acceptance test is whether it can diagnose its own birth. The session brainstormed ultralearn, executed /ultrapowers (twice, with a salvage) to build it, then immediately began dogfooding the finished harvester on the very transcript that built it — and the harvester's mis-extractions (planPath placeholder, transcriptDir garbage) were predicted in advance and then observed. The harvester cannot distinguish a session that EXECUTES /ultrapowers from one that DEVELOPS/DOCUMENTS it (45 slash_ultrapowers mentions, mostly prose).
implication: The deepest frontier signal: a deterministic harvester built on heuristic text extractors is structurally fooled by self-referential sessions, and this was caught only because the operator dogfooded immediately. The design did not anticipate that documentation-dense sessions are a distinct adversarial input class. ultralearn needs a 'meta-session' classifier (or extractors anchored on tool_use/tool_result structure, not prose) so the corpus of ultrapowers-development sessions doesn't poison its own ledger. This is the highest-altitude lesson of the run.
title: Self-referential recursion: /ultrapowers used to build the tool that learns from /ultrapowers runs, validated on its own birth transcript

evidence: The fifth dimension reframes the whole thing. The TDD-decline seed is an emergent behavior — a planning agent self-limiting on a principled constraint. It tells me the sensor can't just be a fixed checklist of failure counters — it needs an open-ended 'what's novel or surprising here?' reader alongside the structured extractors. The agent generalized a single seed example into a first-class 'frontier' lens, and noticed the observation lives in planning dialogue (layer 1) that no metrics pipeline catches.
implication: The design process itself exhibited the right altitude: refusing to reduce qualitative emergent behavior to counters, and recognizing the corpus spans the whole lifecycle (brainstorm->plan->route->execute->gate), not just execution. This validates the five-lens (incl. open-ended frontier) rubric as built. Capture as a positive design-reasoning exemplar; no fix.
title: The brainstorming agent declined a fixed-checklist sensor design, demanding an open-ended 'what's novel?' reader

evidence: Operator's distill table, Finding 5: T5 test asserted 'merge_ledger.py' but the prescribed body didn't contain it — an authoring slip. Also a positive: the implementer correctly resolved toward the test (suite = authority) — worth documenting as intended behavior. The implementer reconciled an inconsistent plan by trusting the test rather than the prose, an unprompted self-correction.
implication: An emergent, design-aligned behavior: a worktree-isolated implementer resolved a plan contradiction by treating the committed test as ground truth (DRY/TDD discipline holding under ambiguity). This is exactly the kind of frontier signal the design wanted to surface and is worth documenting as intended TDD-arbitration behavior. The authoring slip itself argues for a writing-plans/ultraplan self-review check: every test asserting a literal must trace to prescribed content.
title: A plan-internal test/content contradiction was self-corrected by the implementer toward the test as authority

evidence: The shipped harvester scans all projects and detects every real run — there's no single-session input flag yet. We target this one by filtering to its runId after the scan. (That missing affordance is arguably an 8th finding, but it's minor.) The operator had to compute coordinates by hand (session uuid 21215707..., runId a6afa3bef85c5fa2) to point at one session.
implication: harvest_runs.py only supports a full corpus scan; pointing ultralearn at one named session requires manual runId filtering. Add a --session / --project targeting flag so 'run ultralearn on session X' is a first-class operation rather than scan-then-filter. Minor but recurring ergonomics gap that the operator flagged unprompted.
title: Harvester offers no single-session targeting flag — runs are filtered by runId post-scan

evidence: The orchestrator completed full dependency analysis, knob derivation, and fired the saved workflow (a real run-id and transcript dir came back, the run reported itself 'live and headless'). Only afterward did it surface that the harness-reported session was NOT a git repository: the human had invoked the skill from a CONTAINER directory whose child holds the actual repo. Worktree isolation binds to the session repo, so a launch from a non-repo cwd has nothing to bind to. The orchestrator had to abort, clean up a stray no-op branch, and instruct the human to exit and restart a fresh session from the real repo subdirectory before re-invoking. No preflight caught the non-repo cwd before the (write-side) launch.
implication: Add a hard Step-1 preflight that asserts the session cwd is inside a git work tree (and resolves to the repo root, not a parent container) BEFORE any dependency analysis or workflow launch. A non-repo or container-parent cwd must stop the run with the restart instruction, never reach a 'launched' state. The invocation-context note in SKILL.md states the precondition but nothing enforces it.
title: Engine launched a headless run from a session NOT rooted in the git repo; worktree binding could not succeed

evidence: Knob derivation went well beyond static reading: the orchestrator noticed that one mid-pipeline task removes a symbol that a later-wave task still references, so the integrated tree would be transiently red between those two wave merges and would spuriously cascade-block all downstream waves. Rather than guess, it executed the real test suite to measure the exact blast radius (which integration test actually goes red vs which runs in a stubbed/skip mode), established a green unit baseline, probed whether fresh worktrees would have language/runtime deps and how they get provisioned, and only then set a narrowed per-task test command so the transient breakage would not cascade. This is design-unanticipated self-instrumentation at plan-compile time.
implication: This is a positive exemplar worth encoding as guidance: when a plan's own task ordering implies a transient red between two wave merges, the compiler/orchestrator should detect the symbol-removed-before-rewrite pattern and recommend narrowing the run-wide test command (or a per-task override) so the merge gate does not cascade-block. Consider surfacing this empirical-confirmation step in the dependency-analysis reference as a sanctioned pre-launch check.
title: Orchestrator empirically ran the live suite to confirm a transient between-waves breakage before deriving the test-command knob

evidence: The audit block shows only setup/probe-scale agents (tens of turns, low thousands of output tokens) because no waves ran. But the orchestrator's own pre-launch work was heavy: full compiler run, references reading, live baseline suite execution, transient-breakage investigation, and launch-artifact generation — all wasted when the run aborted on the non-repo cwd and had to restart cold in a new session. The expensive analysis is not captured in the agent audit at all (it lived in the main session), so the true cost of the false start is invisible to the effort audit.
implication: Two implications: (1) the cwd preflight above would have moved this cost to the very front, before any analysis; (2) the effort-audit script only sees sub-agent transcripts, so a run that dies in the main session before dispatch reads as near-zero cost — the audit understates aborted/false-start runs. Consider noting that the main-session analysis cost is out of audit scope.
title: Substantial pre-launch analysis tokens spent on a run that never executed a single wave

evidence: Same plan as a prior attempt, re-run from the correct repo. The saved workflow launched by name threw at its own args validation in milliseconds (zero agents, repo untouched) because the args object did not populate the script globals. The orchestrator ran a read-only diagnostic workflow with the IDENTICAL payload delivered as an inline script and it arrived perfectly (correct array, all keys). It then shrank the payload progressively down to a minimal size; all three name-launched attempts failed identically while inline delivery kept working. Conclusion: the saved-workflow-by-name launch path was not delivering args in this environment, independent of payload size. This matched a documented engine-drift fallback condition.
implication: The by-name saved-workflow launch surface is fragile for non-trivial arg payloads in some environments, and there is no automatic detection before the real launch. The zero-agent preflight probe should be extended to carry a representative (non-tiny) args payload and assert it round-trips through the BY-NAME path specifically, so the args-delivery failure is caught at preflight rather than after three failed real-launch attempts. The tiny-probe-passes-but-real-payload-drops asymmetry is the trap.
title: Saved-workflow-by-name launch silently failed to deliver args three times; identical payload arrived intact via an inline-script workflow

evidence: When the parallel launch path proved unusable, the orchestrator had a tempting escape: it had ALREADY proven that an inline-script workflow delivered the same args perfectly, so it could have authored an ad-hoc inline workflow to drive the run. It explicitly declined, citing the determinism guard that forbids improvising a write-side workflow at runtime, and instead routed to the sanctioned sequential fallback. The design's prohibition survived a moment where the agent had both motive and a demonstrated mechanism to violate it.
implication: Positive exemplar — the write/read boundary and determinism guard worked as intended at exactly the failure mode they were written for. Worth preserving as a calibration example: an agent that had empirically shown an inline path works still must not promote it to a write-side substitute. No change needed; consider citing as a guard-held case.
title: Determinism guard held under pressure: orchestrator refused to hand-author a write-side substitute and fell back to the sanctioned sequential executor

evidence: Earlier in planning the operator had explicitly selected the parallel path (and earlier still declined the sequential one). When the engine made the parallel path impossible, the orchestrator did NOT silently downgrade to sequential; it surfaced the diagnosis and posed an explicit choice, noting 'this is the execution mode you explicitly chose against earlier, so I won't switch silently.' The operator then chose the sequential fallback. This is the autonomy posture working: a forced mode change that contradicts a prior operator decision is escalated, not absorbed.
implication: Good behavior to keep. Reinforces that a between-mode switch (parallel to sequential) caused by engine failure is a catastrophe-class escalation point even mid-run, because it reverses an explicit operator preference. Consider naming 'reverses a prior operator mode choice' as an explicit escalation trigger in the autonomy-posture guidance.
title: Orchestrator stopped to ask rather than silently switch execution modes the operator had previously chosen against

evidence: The orchestrator noticed (a 'useful surprise') that the harness reported the session as non-git, yet the real repo resolved one directory level down, and the session-start install had written the saved workflows into the OUTER container's workflow directory — so the engine's saved-workflow registry lived at the container root while the repo (where worktrees must bind) was a child. It had to investigate how the engine binds to the repo before launching anything write-side. Same container-vs-repo mismatch family as the other run in this project.
implication: When the session cwd is a parent container of the actual repo, the session-start install and the engine registry land at the wrong level relative to where worktrees bind. The cwd-is-repo-root preflight (above) should also verify the saved-workflow install landed in the SAME tree the worktrees will bind to, not a parent. This is a recurring failure shape across this project's runs.
title: Saved-workflow registry installed into the outer container's config dir while the git repo sat one level down

evidence: The plan compiled cleanly into a multi-wave parallel shape with zero marker conflicts, all task types trusted verbatim, and disjoint per-task file ownership — a textbook good ultrapowers candidate. The routing call to go parallel was correct on the merits. Yet the run could not execute in parallel for a reason wholly unrelated to plan shape (engine args-delivery). The handoff/routing rubric scores plan suitability but has no signal for 'engine can actually launch this here.'
implication: Routing suitability and engine deliverability are orthogonal; a perfect routing recommendation can still be unexecutable. The preflight that validates the by-name launch path with a real payload (above) is what closes this gap — routing should not be second-guessed when the only failure is delivery. No change to the routing heuristic itself; the fix is the preflight.
title: Routing recommendation was sound (marked plan, clean parallel waves) but undeliverable; recommendation quality and engine deliverability are separate axes

evidence: This bundle contains NO ultrapowers content whatsoever — no wave plan, no integration branch, no per-task review, no compiler output, no plan path (planPath is null, planningFound false). The session is general domain work that invoked a DIFFERENT named Workflow (a web-research fan-out harness) whose transcript dir matches the generic workflows/wf_* path the harvester keys on. The harvester bundled it purely on that path pattern. The large audit block (around 100 same-model agents, ~1150 turns, ~160K output tokens) is the research harness's fan-out, not ultrapowers waves — which is why every agent role reads as 'unknown' and no ultrapowers structure exists. A reader applying the five ultrapowers lenses to this bundle is reading the wrong kind of run.
implication: The harvester must filter workflow runs by WHICH workflow produced them — match the script/run identity (the ultrapowers-run harness name) rather than the generic workflows/wf_* transcript-dir pattern, which catches every Workflow-tool invocation in a project (research harnesses, ad-hoc inline workflows, etc.). Without this, the ledger is polluted with foreign-workflow runs and the audit/cost numbers are meaningless for ultrapowers. Highest-value fix in this batch.
title: Harvester misclassified a non-ultrapowers Workflow run (a research fan-out) as an ultrapowers run

evidence: The embedded research harness (which advertises an adversarial claim-verification step) aggregated sources and assigned one a top-credibility tier. The aggregation had conflated that source with a different document; the actual document was off-topic and did not support the claimed purpose. The harness's own verify stage did not catch the mis-categorization — the human did, on review, and the orchestrator owned the error and struck it, noting nothing in the aggregated list is trustworthy until the underlying document is actually read. So a multi-agent fan-out with a built-in verify gate still emitted a confidently-tiered but wrong artifact.
implication: Even though this is a foreign workflow, the pattern is directly relevant to ultrapowers' own verification thesis: an adversarial/critic stage that reviews aggregated CLAIMS rather than re-deriving each from the primary artifact can rubber-stamp a confident-but-wrong synthesis. For ultrapowers' completeness critic and any future research/triage harness, the lesson is that verification must touch the primary artifact (read the file / run the test), not just review a summary of it. Reinforces the ledger's 'verify against the artifact, not the narrative' principle.
title: A fan-out research workflow propagated an unverified, wrongly-categorized source past its own adversarial-verify stage; the human caught it, not the verifier

evidence: The audit shows roughly 100 agents on one model, ~1150 total turns and ~160K output tokens, every single one role 'unknown' and misrankCandidates empty. Because this is a foreign research fan-out (see the misclassification finding), none of these numbers map onto ultrapowers roles (implementer/reviewer/merge/completeness), so no tier-misrank or parallelism-payoff signal can be drawn. The audit's role-inference produced 'unknown' for the entire population, which is itself the tell that the run is not ultrapowers-shaped.
implication: When the audit cannot assign ANY known ultrapowers role to a run's agents (100% 'unknown'), that is a strong signal the bundle is not an ultrapowers run and should be excluded by the harvester (ties to the misclassification fix). Secondarily, the audit/harvest could flag '100% unknown roles' as a quarantine condition rather than emitting a bundle that downstream readers must waste effort discovering is foreign.
title: Audit block is large but uninterpretable for ultrapowers because the run is foreign; ~100 agents all tagged role 'unknown'

evidence: In a multi-wave parallel run, one implementer produced correct, committed work on its task branch but a transient structured-output return failure prevented that branch from being merged into the integration branch. No task was marked failed and no wave was marked blocked; the dropped merge surfaced only because the end-of-run completeness critic independently re-checked the integrated tree and found the expected change absent. The same flake later hit a single-task preflight agent on a follow-up wave, aborting it before any mutation.
implication: A structured-output flake on an implementer should not be able to drop a merge silently. The merge/completeness path needs an explicit reconciliation that diffs each task branch's expected change against the integration tree and raises a first-class 'dropped-merge' status (not just a critic prose note), so the gate sees it as a failed/unfinished task rather than relying on the critic to notice. Consider an automatic bounded retry on the transient structured-output flake before declaring the agent done.
title: A transient 'subagent completed without calling StructuredOutput' flake silently dropped a finished task's merge

evidence: Every per-task verdict was clean and the suite passed, yet the integrated tree was missing one task's change because its merge had been dropped. The independent completeness critic, reviewing the integration tree end-to-end against the plan rather than per-task, located exactly the one missing change and named it as the single real gap. This is the verification-by-something-it-can't-touch design catching a class of failure (lost merge) that none of the producing agents could self-report.
implication: Strong exemplar: the completeness critic earns its cost precisely on failures the per-task layer is structurally blind to. Worth hardening into a guaranteed branch-vs-tree reconciliation rather than leaving it to the critic's prose, and worth citing as the canonical case for keeping an independent integration-level critic.
title: End-of-run completeness critic caught a silently dropped merge that every per-task signal reported as clean

evidence: During plan authoring/compile, the compiler derived an interface symbol from the leading non-code token of a 'Consumes'/'Produces' bullet. Several bullets led with a common article, so the compiler matched that identical stop-word across unrelated tasks and emitted false producer->consumer edges, wrongly pushing an independent task out of the first parallel wave and serializing it. The operator had to dump the compiler's edge reasoning, diagnose that the matched symbol was the article, and rewrite every interface bullet to lead with a backticked symbol before the waves came out correct.
implication: The interface-symbol extractor must ignore stop-words / require a code-formatted (backticked) token before treating a Consumes/Produces lead as a dependency symbol; otherwise prose articles create phantom edges that needlessly serialize parallelizable work. Either tighten extraction or have ultraplan enforce backticked-symbol-first in interface bullets at authoring time.
title: Plan compiler invented spurious dependency edges by extracting a bare stop-word as an interface symbol

evidence: The plan used a non-'### Task' heading style for its end-of-plan gate sections. Because those headings are not recognized as task boundaries, the compiler attributed their stray Type/Depends-on marker lines to the last real implementation task that textually preceded them, producing 'stray marker' conflict notes the operator had to inspect and dismiss. This same pattern recurred across two separate plan-compile passes in this project (the operator eventually converted the gate headings to proper task-shaped headings to clear it).
implication: Compiler should recognize the gate heading form as a section boundary so trailing markers are not mis-attributed to the prior task, or ultraplan should standardize gates as task-shaped headings. Surface this class as an authoring lint, not a per-run conflict note the operator must triage every time.
title: Gate/non-task headings leaked their markers into the textually preceding implementation task (recurs across runs)

evidence: A single run executed roughly two dozen implementation tasks across three waves, with the first wave running ~17 tasks in parallel (above the engine concurrency cap, so a few queued). Nearly all tasks landed clean on the first pass; exactly one needed a single fix iteration. Same-file pairs were correctly sequenced by dependency markers so no two same-wave tasks touched the same file, and the integrated suite went green. This is a high-end demonstration of the parallel path holding at scale.
implication: Calibration data point: the design scales to ~17-wide waves and ~two-dozen tasks with near-zero rework when the plan's writes are disjoint and same-file pairs are sequenced. Reinforces that disjoint-writes + explicit same-file sequencing is the precondition for clean wide fan-out; worth stating as the planning target.
title: Very large fan-out (22 tasks / 17-wide first wave) succeeded with only one fix iteration

evidence: The effort audit flagged a single tier-misrank candidate: a task assigned the cheapest model tier consumed by far the most turns of any agent in the run (well over 100 turns, roughly double the next implementer), yet still landed clean. The operator read this as a signal to bump that task's tier next time rather than a defect in this run.
implication: The misrank-candidate signal works as intended (cheap tier + outlier turn count => underscoped). Feeding flagged tasks forward into the next plan's tier assignment is the right loop; consider making the audit emit a concrete 'suggest tier bump' hint keyed to the task identity so it is actionable without operator interpretation.
title: A cheap-tier implementer burned far more turns than its tier, flagged as the run's only tier-misrank

evidence: Across the session the operator's attention concentrated exactly where the design intends: authoring/marking the plan, auditing the rendered wave interpretation, and deciding at the pre-merge gate (the operator chose Approve, then later Salvage for the dropped-merge case). After the first run shipped, the operator immediately brainstormed, spec'd, marked, compiled, and executed a second plan in the same session, treating the engine as a repeatable fan-out tool rather than a one-shot.
implication: Confirms the intended attention shift (humans on planning + the single gate, not implementation). The friction the operator did absorb was compile-time marker debugging, not implementation review — which argues for moving that cost upstream into ultraplan authoring lints.
title: Operator stayed in planning/gate attention and ran a second full plan-author->compile->execute cycle in one session

evidence: At the gate the completeness critic both asserted that a project-wide typecheck exited clean and, in the same report, listed a concrete type error as a real gap. The operator did not trust the relayed claim: he read the integration branch directly to independently confirm both gaps were real (one a dropped design clause, one a stale test-fixture literal) and to establish that the critic's 'typecheck clean' line was simply wrong. The gate decision was unaffected because acceptance bound to the actual test suite, not the critic's prose.
implication: The completeness critic's free-text claims are not internally consistent and cannot be taken at face value. The report format should separate machine-checkable assertions (typecheck/suite exit codes, run deterministically) from the critic's prose findings, so a contradictory narrative line can't masquerade as a verification. Until then, the gate guidance to independently ground critic claims against the branch is load-bearing and should be explicit.
title: Completeness critic emitted a self-contradiction (claimed a typecheck passed while reporting a real type error), forcing the operator to re-ground gaps by hand

evidence: The completeness critic noted two beneficial deviations from the written plan: an implementer's helper diverged from the plan's exact pseudocode in a way that fixed a latent off-by-one the literal code would have shipped, and another implementer correctly dropped a plan-specified clause that, taken literally, would have force-recreated a never-initialized resource and broken a test. The critic also noted the dropped clause was an over-correction (it lost a secondary behavior), which became the Redirect target. Agents reasoned about plan correctness rather than transcribing it.
implication: Implementers exercising judgment against a flawed plan is a feature, but it widens the gap between 'what the plan said' and 'what shipped', which is exactly what the completeness critic must surface precisely. Argues for the critic explicitly classifying each deviation as beneficial / neutral / regressive so the gate can weigh them — and for capturing beneficial deviations back into the plan-authoring guidance.
title: Implementers improved on the plan's literal pseudocode — one fixed a latent off-by-one, another correctly declined a plan clause that would have broken a case

evidence: In a repo with a nested subproject, the saved-workflow install step deposited byte-for-byte copies of the engine workflow scripts into the nested subproject's workflow directory in addition to the repo root. These copies belonged only at the root (which is gitignored); the nested ones were untracked clutter unrelated to the work, and the operator manually removed them before committing to avoid polluting the change.
implication: The install glob is over-broad in repos with nested subprojects: it should target only the session repo root's workflow directory, or the cleanup/sweep step should remove stray installed copies outside the root. Otherwise every run in a monorepo-shaped tree leaves artifacts the operator has to notice and delete by hand.
title: Harness install scattered byte-copies of the saved-workflow scripts into a nested subproject directory, leaving stray artifacts to clean up

evidence: The run passed both the test and acceptance gates, but the completeness critic surfaced two real gaps that did not fail the suite. The operator chose Redirect: two precise corrective tasks (one given two adversarial review passes because it sat on a subtle trap, one lean) were relaunched with resume onto the existing integration branch as one short parallel wave, merged, and the clean-tree + HEAD-match guards re-passed. The pass-the-gate-but-still-improve path worked deterministically.
implication: Positive exemplar for the Redirect-on-clean-gate path: the gate's value is not only blocking bad merges but enabling targeted refinement on a green run. Confirms the design intent that real-but-non-failing critic findings get a deterministic, bounded corrective wave rather than a full re-run.
title: Redirect cleanly closed two non-failing-but-real gaps on the same integration branch without a full re-run

evidence: At the gate the operator explicitly declined to act on the critic's relayed assertions, instead reading the integration branch directly to confirm each gap and to catch the critic's contradictory claim, before choosing Redirect. Post-merge the operator also drove a live browser smoke test of the shipped behavior rather than trusting the green suite alone. The attention spent was almost entirely at planning and the gate, matching the design.
implication: The operator has internalized 'verify critic claims against the tree' — the design should make that cheaper, not rely on operator diligence: surface the critic's machine-checkable claims as re-runnable commands at the gate so grounding is a click, not a manual code-read.
title: Operator distrusts relayed critic claims and verifies against the branch before deciding — a learned, healthy gate posture

evidence: This bundle is not an ultrapowers parallel-execution run at all: it is an improvised, read-only multi-agent research/dossier workflow (scope -> parallel web-search fan-out -> fetch/extract -> adversarial multi-vote verification -> synthesis). The bundle has no plan path, no waves, no gate report, no integration branch, and its audit tagged every one of its agents with role 'unknown' (no implementer/reviewer/merge structure exists to detect). It was harvested only because it is a Workflow run in a project adjacent to ultrapowers use.
implication: The harvester should distinguish ultrapowers execution runs (waves + gate + integration branch) from improvised read-only dynamic workflows and either exclude the latter or tag them as a separate kind, so they don't dilute the run ledger or feed all-'unknown' degraded audit rows into the lenses. A planPath==null + no-wave-structure run is the detectable signature.
title: Harvester ingested a non-ultrapowers improvised read-only research workflow as if it were an execution run, yielding a degraded bundle

evidence: The audit block for this run lists every agent with role 'unknown' (because the run carries none of the implementer/reviewer/merge labels the role-tagger keys on) and an empty misrank-candidate list. Total effort is recorded, but it cannot be attributed to roles or checked for tier-misranks, so the cost lens has nothing actionable to say about this run beyond raw token/turn totals.
implication: Role-tagging and misrank detection assume the ultrapowers role vocabulary; against an improvised workflow they degrade to all-'unknown' with no signal. Either gate the audit to ultrapowers-shaped runs, or teach the tagger to recognize generic workflow phase labels so non-ultrapowers runs at least get phase-level attribution instead of a wall of 'unknown'.
title: Audit produced no usable cost signal for the improvised workflow: all roles 'unknown', no misrank detection possible

evidence: The improvised workflow fanned out parallel search agents and ran a multi-vote adversarial verification step over extracted claims (a quorum of refutes required to kill a claim) before synthesizing a cited report — substantial autonomous multi-agent work — yet it never created branches, merged, or mutated a repository. It is exactly the read-only / write-side split the design reserves for improvised workflows: nothing improvised held merge keys.
implication: Confirms the read/write boundary holds in practice: a richly capable improvised workflow operated entirely read-only. Useful evidence that the policy distinction (improvised => read-only; write-side => registry harness only) is observed even in foreign projects, and that adversarial-verification topologies are a candidate for the harness ratchet if a write-side analogue is ever wanted.
title: Read-only improvised workflow stayed within the read-only boundary and did real adversarial verification without any merge keys

evidence: A plan carrying full parallel-execution markers was nevertheless routed to the sequential executor by the planning agent's own recommendation. The dependency graph was a mostly-serial spine (one foundation task, a single fan-out wave of disjoint tasks behind it, then a serial integration/gate tail), so the only concurrency on offer was one wave; the planner judged the wall-clock saving small relative to the run's correctness sensitivity. The run then executed sequentially and completed cleanly. Marker presence did not imply the parallel engine was the right call.
implication: Routing guidance should treat marker-presence and parallel-suitability as independent. A marked plan whose shape is a long serial spine with a single narrow fan-out is a weak ultrapowers candidate; the routing hook / ultraplan handoff should name 'mostly-serial spine, one parallel wave' as a signal that the sequential executor may be the better default, rather than letting the recommended-for-marked-plans framing push every marked plan toward parallel.
title: Planning agent declined ultrapowers on a marked plan and chose the sequential executor

evidence: When choosing an execution mode, the planning agent articulated a risk model the design does not encode: when N independent tasks all sit behind one shared foundation task, running them in parallel makes all N inherit the same foundation flaw simultaneously and forces the same bug to be debugged N times across N isolated worktrees, whereas sequential execution surfaces the flaw once (ideally on the hardest dependent first), fixes the foundation, and propagates the fix. It explicitly preferred proving the foundation against the worst-case dependent before fanning out.
implication: ultrapowers' wave model assumes same-wave tasks are independent, but tasks sharing a freshly-built foundation are correlated through that foundation's correctness. Consider a documented heuristic (in the lenses / routing guidance) that when a fan-out wave depends on a just-authored shared contract, either serialize a single canary dependent first or warn the operator that a foundation bug will replicate across the whole wave. This is a genuine gap between 'no file conflict' independence and 'no shared-risk' independence.
title: Agent reasoned that parallelism amplifies a shared-foundation bug while sequential execution learns from the first instance

evidence: A run executed with acceptance waived (operator-approved reasoning: the build's own strict structural gate plus human fidelity review were deemed sufficient). At the pre-merge gate the full repo test suite was RED: a pre-existing test hardcoded a total-count constant and an enumerated set that the new work changed, and no task in the plan had been assigned to update that guard. The integration review flagged it as a plan/coverage gap, not an implementation slip. The operator fixed the few mechanical lines by hand at the gate and re-ran green.
implication: When acceptance is waived, the safety net leans entirely on the existing repo suite — but additive work that changes a global count/enumeration commonly invalidates a hardcoded guard that no task touches. ultraplan self-review (or the compiler's transparency render) should surface 'this work changes a project-wide count/set; is there a guard test that needs an owning task?' as a check, so the gap is caught at authoring time rather than discovered RED at the gate.
title: Waived acceptance let a red repo suite reach the pre-merge gate because no task owned a hardcoded-count guard update

evidence: Two tasks in the same parallel wave, each writing its own isolated file with no file overlap, independently authored an entity with the same globally-unique name. Worktree isolation hid the collision until the integration task built the merged artifact, where the global-uniqueness invariant failed. The integration agent self-resolved: kept one as canonical, renamed the other with a disambiguating suffix, and updated that entity's internal referrers — recording the resolution as a judgment call. The design's file-overlap dependency inference cannot see this class of conflict because the namespace is shared but the files are not.
implication: Worktree isolation prevents file-level conflicts but not shared-namespace conflicts (globally-unique identifiers minted independently in parallel). ultrapowers/ultraplan has no signal for 'these parallel tasks mint into a shared global namespace.' Document this as a known limitation and consider an authoring rule: when parallel tasks populate a globally-unique keyspace, assign disjoint prefixes/ranges per task, or expect the integration task to own deduplication. The integration agent absorbing it as a judgment call worked here but is fragile.
title: Integration agent resolved a cross-task global-uniqueness collision at merge time that two parallel tasks independently created

evidence: A task's prescribed final step invoked a review-packaging helper script by a repo-relative path that exists only in the engine's own source tree, not in the target project being operated on. The script and its directory were absent from both the worktree and the main checkout. The agent recorded a judgment call and hand-wrote an equivalent review packet (commit list + diff with context) to a shared location instead of failing.
implication: A baked task/prompt step referenced a helper that is only guaranteed to exist inside the engine repo, not in an arbitrary target project. Any prescribed shell step the engine bakes into task bodies must either ship with the run, be resolved from the engine install path, or degrade gracefully — relying on a path that happens to exist only when the target IS the engine repo is a latent failure for every foreign run. Audit baked prompts/scripts for target-repo path assumptions.
title: A prescribed engine helper script was absent in the foreign repo; the task agent improvised an equivalent

evidence: In a fan-out-then-converge run, the single Wave-2 integration agent consumed roughly an order of magnitude more turns and far more output tokens than any individual fan-out agent — it stitched all cross-links, resolved the namespace collision, ran the strict build, and curated metadata. The convergence step, not the parallel fan-out, was the cost and latency bottleneck; the parallel wave's per-agent cost was modest by comparison.
implication: The parallelism payoff narrative focuses on the fan-out wave, but in converge-heavy plans the lone integration task is the critical path for both wall-clock and tokens and runs on the most capable tier. ultraplan guidance could flag heavyweight single integration tasks as candidates for decomposition into smaller, independently-reviewable convergence steps, and the cost lens should explicitly report 'integration-task share of total' since a big convergence step erodes the parallel speedup.
title: One integration task dominated the run at ~5x the next agent's turns and tokens

evidence: Before any planning or wave execution, the run was gated on two external preconditions: a system runtime below the required version floor needing a privileged installer (no passwordless escalation available, so the operator had to run it in their own terminal), and an external application connection that required a GUI action and an app relaunch. The agent did all the filesystem legwork, staged everything, and stopped at the gate. On the first hand-back the operator completed the GUI half but skipped the privileged install; the agent detected the still-old version from the package receipt and re-prompted with the exact one-line command, and only then proceeded.
implication: Environment/toolchain preconditions that need privilege or GUI are real manual gates that block the whole run, and operators silently skip steps. This is exactly the 'manual' task class, but here it preceded planning rather than living in a post-merge runbook. ultraplan/SKILL guidance should encourage hoisting environment-precondition checks into an explicit early manual gate with verifiable evidence (re-read the version/receipt, don't trust the operator's 'done'), and the agent verifying via the receipt rather than the operator's word is a good exemplar worth codifying.
title: Up-front manual gate (privileged runtime install + GUI connection setup) stalled before any design or execution began

evidence: The run's audit block listed exactly one misrankCandidate — an implementer on the most-capable tier that ran roughly 2-3x the turns and tokens of the median agent. The agent role labels also showed several roles appearing twice (the same task index attached to two different model tiers), consistent with re-dispatch / fix iterations that the flat audit cannot cleanly attribute. The misrank surfaced a task that was heavier than its tier assignment implied.
implication: The misrank detector is doing useful work (a single outlier flagged), but duplicated role labels show the audit cannot distinguish an original implementer from its re-dispatch/fix iteration, blurring per-task cost attribution. Strengthen the audit/harvest to disambiguate re-dispatched agents from originals (e.g. iteration index per role) so cost-per-task and misrank signals are trustworthy.
title: Effort audit flagged a misrank candidate: one implementer ran far more turns/tokens than its tier-peers

evidence: A run completed with all tasks done, all reviews clean, suites green, and the pre-merge gate approved. When the operator ran the resulting artifact end-to-end, the output was qualitatively disappointing — correct and faithful to the spec, but flat/uninteresting against the operator's stated creative intent. The gate verified everything verifiable headlessly and explicitly deferred the experiential quality to a manual run, where the real shortfall lived. The operator's critique was about felt quality, not correctness, and the system had no place to register it.
implication: For creative/experiential work the verification regime certifies correctness but is structurally blind to whether the artifact achieves its qualitative goal — exactly where the operator's attention should land. ultrapowers keeps the human at planning and the pre-merge gate, but for aesthetic work there is a missing post-merge 'experiential acceptance' loop. Acceptance disposition guidance should name 'felt quality cannot be sealed or suite-tested' explicitly and route it to a mandatory manual evaluation step in the runbook, not bury it under a generic waiver.
title: Artifact passed every gate yet failed the operator's actual aesthetic goal when run for real

evidence: At the pre-merge gate the agent reported two behaviors that exactly matched the approved plan's own reference code but were arguably not what the operator ultimately wanted — one a substantive quality refinement, one a trivial cleanup — and explicitly presented them as 'faithful to the plan, but yours to weigh' rather than either silently shipping them or treating plan-fidelity as a defect. The operator then chose to redirect and fix both before merge. This is the design's plan-mandated-finding philosophy realized: the agent neither graded its own plan nor overrode the human.
implication: Positive exemplar: the gate distinguishing 'faithful-but-maybe-undesired' from 'correctness defect' and handing the call to the human is exactly the intended division of attention. Worth capturing in report-format guidance as the model for presenting plan-faithful divergences — name the behavior, cite that it mirrors the plan, recommend, and let the operator weigh.
title: Gate surfaced plan-faithful divergences as explicit operator decisions rather than silently passing or failing them

evidence: Reaching a merged state took the initial run plus two resume-onto-the-same-integration-branch redirect cycles (one to fix the divergences, and post-merge a packaging defect where the distributable archive omitted required runtime directories). Across these, the gate's clean-tree guard repeatedly tripped on benign gitignored install output and lockfile churn left on the session checkout by review/test agents running dependency installs; each time the agent correctly refused to silently wipe it and had to manually confirm it was install leftover rather than unreviewed source.
implication: Review/test agents running dependency installs on the session checkout leave gitignored install output and tracked-lockfile churn that repeatedly trips the gate's clean-tree guard, costing a manual confirmation each time. The engine should either run installs inside the worktrees only, or teach the clean-tree guard to classify known-gitignored install artifacts and lockfile churn as benign automatically, so the operator isn't asked to adjudicate the same leftover on every redirect.
title: Two resume/redirect cycles plus repeated clean-tree-guard trips on gitignored install churn around the gate

evidence: During planning, when the operator challenged a recommendation, the agent acknowledged it had quietly recommended along a reliability/demo-safety axis after the operator had explicitly named a different optimization goal a few turns earlier, conceded the bias, and re-recommended on the operator's actual stated criterion. The operator's intent and the agent's reflexive default had diverged silently until the operator caught it.
implication: A recurring operator-trust hazard: the planning agent drifts from the operator's stated optimization axis back to a generic engineering default without flagging it, so the human must police it. ultraplan/brainstorming guidance could prompt the agent to restate the operator's named optimization criterion at each recommendation point, so recommendations are explicitly scored against the operator's axis rather than a reflexive default.
title: Planning agent caught itself substituting an engineer's default for the operator's stated optimization criterion

evidence: In contrast to the marked-but-serial plan that was routed away from the parallel engine, this plan decomposed into a multi-wave shape with a real fan-out of independent components behind a foundation, and ultrapowers executed all waves with worktree isolation and per-task review, every task merging clean (one self-corrected in a single fix iteration). The wave shape the compiler computed matched the intended decomposition, and marker parsing was trusted with no heuristic guessing.
implication: Positive exemplar confirming the routing intuition from the contrasting run: ultrapowers shines when the fan-out is genuinely independent components, not a thin parallel pocket on a serial spine. Pairing this clean fit against the serial-spine plan that was routed to sequential gives a concrete two-point calibration for the routing heuristic — 'independent components behind a foundation' = parallel; 'serial spine, one narrow wave' = sequential.
title: Genuinely-independent fan-out plan was a strong ultrapowers fit and ran clean across four waves

evidence: The repository was healthy git (rev-parse and manual worktree-add both succeeded for the orchestrator), but the host harness had cached an 'is a git repository: false' determination at session start because the repo was initialized AFTER the session began. The engine therefore reported it could not create per-task worktrees and demanded worktree-create hooks. No tasks ran, nothing merged. Recurs across 2 runs in this project (same root cause hit a later run on a different plan).
implication: The skill should add an early, explicit preflight that runs a live `git rev-parse`/`git worktree add` smoke test in the session and compares it to the harness's cached git-status banner; on mismatch, warn the operator that a session restart is required BEFORE compiling and launching, rather than discovering it only after a no-op run consumed setup. A documented recovery note (restart cures it) should be surfaced at that preflight.
title: Stale session-start git detection disables native worktree isolation, aborting the launch

evidence: After the launch aborted on worktree creation, the run still surfaced a passing test/acceptance signal — but that signal was computed against the unchanged pre-implementation tree (only the plan-doc commit present), so all passing tests were pre-existing. The orchestrator recognized the green was vacuous (integration HEAD equalled baseline), refused to treat it as done, and diagnosed the environment rather than rubber-stamping. This is exactly the design's intended skepticism, achieved without a mechanical guard firing.
implication: Promote this from emergent judgment to a mechanical gate: the pre-merge step should hard-compare integration HEAD against the recorded baseline sha and refuse 'Approve' when they are equal (no work merged), independent of the test/acceptance booleans. The design already verifies HEAD==last-merge-headSha; it should also verify HEAD != baseline before honoring a pass.
title: Orchestrator self-corrected a vacuous 'green' acceptance after a no-op run instead of trusting the passing signal

evidence: The human spent attention exactly where the design intends — choosing build approach, acceptance disposition (waive vs seal for an infra-only first phase), and even naming the product — and authorized the parallel run. But the parallel engine never executed; the operator ended up steering an inline sequential build instead. The attention investment in planning paid off; the promised shift away from implementation attention did not materialize for this run.
implication: When the parallel path aborts pre-execution for an environment reason, the skill should make the fallback decision a crisp operator choice (restart-for-parallel vs proceed-inline-now) with the tradeoff stated, rather than drifting into an inline build that quietly forfeits the parallelism the operator opted into.
title: Operator engaged heavily at planning/naming/disposition but the run never produced parallel value

evidence: The compiler serialized an otherwise-independent task because its Files entries were directory globs rather than concrete file paths (flagged as an ambiguous-files conflict), and a sibling task's Files line carried a non-path identifier token (a function/symbol name in parentheses) that was dropped from overlap inference. Both are authoring-shape issues that degraded an intended-parallel wave to sequential. Markers naming the manual scaffold task were also correctly dropped because that task was outside the implementation set.
implication: The plan-authoring guidance and self-review checklist should more loudly flag glob Files entries and parenthetical identifier annotations on Files lines, since both silently cost parallelism. Consider a compiler lint that, for a task whose ONLY overlap edge is glob-ambiguity, emits a targeted 'list concrete files to parallelize' hint at the transparency render with the specific paths to expand.
title: Glob/identifier tokens in Files lines forced conservative serialization of an independent task

evidence: A backend framework's git-tracked generated type bindings were never regenerated for the new modules added across the wave, because regeneration requires a live deployment and would clobber parallel worktrees — so it was deliberately deferred to the post-wave gate. The runtime test suite passed fully (hundreds of tests green) because the test harness resolves modules dynamically at runtime, bypassing the stale static bindings; but the typecheck half of the gate failed with many errors referencing the unregenerated modules. The acceptance 'passed' boolean mirrored only the green test run, masking the red typecheck. The completeness critic correctly caught that the two halves of the gate disagreed and that the integrator never ran the failing half.
implication: The engine needs first-class handling for 'codegen deferred to gate' projects: (1) when a run-wide testCmd is deliberately narrowed to the dynamic-resolution-only command, the acceptance/suite boolean must NOT be allowed to read green while a declared gate command (typecheck) is unrun — the gate's full command should be the acceptance authority, or the deferral must be recorded so the pre-merge step KNOWS to run codegen+typecheck before honoring any pass. (2) Document a per-task vs gate split pattern for generated-artifact stacks so the orchestrator wires it deterministically instead of rediscovering it each run.
title: Generated binding/codegen artifacts go stale across parallel worktrees, producing a false-green test run and a red typecheck gate

evidence: Implementation was logically correct and complete against every task; the runtime suite was fully green. The completeness critic nonetheless reconstructed the exact integration tree, ran the typecheck the integrator had skipped, proved it red, and traced precisely why the test runner gave a false-green (runtime module resolution bypassing the stale static bindings) — citing the empty diff of the generated directory as proof codegen never ran. This is the design's independent-verification thesis working at its best: a claim the run made about itself was checked by a role that could not be fooled by the runner's blind spot.
implication: Positive exemplar — preserve and harden. The critic's 'reconstruct the exact tree, run the gate the integrator skipped' move is high-value; ensure the report format elevates a critic BLOCKING finding above a green test boolean so the pre-merge gate can never present 'all tests pass' without the critic's contradicting verdict adjacent to it.
title: Completeness critic detected a false-green masked by a dynamic-import test runner and blocked an otherwise-passing run

evidence: A six-task implementation plan with explicit dependency markers compiled with no marker conflicts into three waves, with a middle wave of three genuinely concurrent tasks and a final wave of two, driven entirely by author-supplied dependency edges. The gate and a deploy/migrate task were correctly classified out into run-config and the post-merge runbook. This is the routing working as intended — clean markers yielded clean parallelism.
implication: Exemplar of well-authored markers. Worth capturing as a reference 'good plan shape' in the authoring docs: concrete file paths per task, explicit Depends-on edges, and impure deploy steps split into a manual runbook task.
title: Marked plan compiled cleanly into a 3-wave DAG with real parallelism and zero marker conflicts

evidence: Every task completed with zero fix iterations and a clean review verdict, yet the audit shows the reviewer/completeness roles (all pinned to the strongest model) accounted for a large share of total turns/tokens — multiple adversarial passes per task plus repeated review re-runs, several reviewers logging more turns than the implementers they reviewed. Roughly half the agent population were review roles on work that needed no correction. The genuine value was concentrated in the single completeness critic that caught the gate failure; the per-task adversarial doubling added little here.
implication: Tune the per-task adversarial heuristic: for routine, low-judgment tasks that pass with no fix loop, two independent review passes are mostly wasted. Consider escalating to the second adversarial pass only on a first-pass FIX_REQUIRED or concern, and lean on the run-level completeness critic (which is where the real defect was found) rather than doubling every task. Surface review-cost-vs-fixes in the audit so operators can see the spend bought no corrections.
title: Heavy adversarial-review spend on implementations that all passed first try with zero fix iterations

evidence: Despite a green runtime suite and an acceptance object reading passed, the orchestrator judged the run not-done (the suite-acceptance failed once the real gate command was considered), recorded a judgment call that the gate must not Approve, and did not hand off. The single human gate held against a misleading self-reported pass — the operator was shown the failure rather than a premature success.
implication: Reinforce: the gate decision must derive from the strongest contradicting signal (critic BLOCKING / real gate command), never from a self-reported acceptance boolean alone. Make the report render the acceptance boolean and the critic verdict side by side so an operator can never approve past a known-red gate.
title: Pre-merge gate correctly withheld Approve on a suite-acceptance false-pass, honoring the human-gate contract

evidence: Across two separate sessions the run was kicked off by a literal 'invoke the workflow with the plan path as args' instruction. The frozen workflow immediately threw its fail-loud validation (it expects compiled waves, not a raw plan path). In each case the orchestrator had to recognize the literal call was a simplification, locate the skill, and run the compile-first multi-step flow by hand — burning turns reading the skill and the workflow source to rediscover the contract. One of these sessions then launched but aborted on the worktree-detection issue, doing no implementation work (a tiny 3-agent run: setup + a probe).
implication: The command/skill entry text presented to the model should not show a one-shot Workflow({args: plan-path}) form that the workflow itself rejects — it trains the wrong first action. Either route the trigger straight into the skill's Step-1 flow, or annotate the shim so the orchestrator compiles first without having to fail, read the source, and reconstruct the intended path.
title: Literal one-shot Workflow invocation in the command shim misleads the orchestrator before it finds the real multi-step flow

evidence: Before launching, the orchestrator empirically established that the runtime test runner works fully offline (proxy + in-memory harness) while codegen needs a live deployment and uploads to a shared backend — so running codegen in parallel worktrees would clobber. It correctly designed the run-wide test command to be runtime-only and deferred typecheck/codegen to the single post-merge gate, and verified a green+clean baseline first. All sound — yet the run then aborted on the cached git-detection issue and produced no work. Good pre-flight reasoning was wasted by an environment fault the skill did not preflight for.
implication: This reinforces the friction finding: the orchestrator can do impressive pre-launch reasoning, but without an early live-git/worktree smoke test that reasoning is spent on a run that cannot execute. Add that smoke test as the first thing after plan confirmation. Also consider letting the operator persist the 'runtime-only testCmd + defer codegen to gate' decision as a project knob so it is not re-derived every session.
title: Orchestrator solved the generated-artifact-vs-worktree clash analytically before launch, then the run still aborted on the environment

evidence: The same plan was compiled, knob-derived, baseline-verified, and launched across two sessions; both aborted at worktree creation with no tasks run (one session's audit shows only setup + a probe agent, ~tens of turns total, no implementation agents). The expensive part of each session was the orchestrator's repeated source-reading and analytical setup, none of which advanced the work — pure rework caused by an environment fault detected too late.
implication: An early worktree smoke test converts this wasted compile/analysis spend into a fast fail with a clear 'restart session' instruction before any compilation, eliminating the duplicated pre-launch cost. Quantify the savings: the orchestrator should not invest in compile + knob derivation + baseline until the worktree path is proven live.
title: Two full pre-launch compile/analysis cycles produced zero implementation across the aborted sessions

evidence: In a parallel run of ~16 tasks, one task's fix branch was authored, committed, and the work was correct, yet that branch was never merged into the integration head. Every per-task reviewer passed; the gap was invisible until the final completeness critic re-detached onto the integration tree and proved via ancestry/diff checks that zero of that task's changes had landed (the file still held the original defect the task existed to remove). The wave-merge step reported MERGED for the wave while omitting that one branch from its merged-branch list — a silent drop, not a surfaced merge failure.
implication: The wave-merge role must reconcile its merged-branch list against the set of tasks that reached 'done' and FAIL LOUD on any task whose branch is missing from the integration ancestry, rather than reporting the wave MERGED. A deterministic post-merge ancestry assertion (every done-task commit is an ancestor of the integration head) belongs in the engine harness, not only in the LLM completeness critic.
title: A completed, correctly-implemented task's branch was silently dropped during wave integration

evidence: The final completeness critic correctly flagged the dropped-task gap and prescribed 'just merge the unmerged branch.' But that branch's content invoked a non-existent API the per-task reviewer had already rejected, so following the critic's advice verbatim would have integrated the rejected, broken implementation. At the human gate the orchestrator cross-checked the branch contents, recognized the critic's fix was unsafe, and instead relaunched a corrected task. A reviewer being right about the defect but wrong about the remedy is a self-correction the design did not explicitly anticipate.
implication: Gate-stage guidance should treat completeness-critic remediation suggestions as advisory, never as auto-apply, and the report format should surface the distinction between 'a gap exists' (high-confidence) and 'here is how to fix it' (must be re-verified). Worth a note in the reviewer/critic prompts that a 'just re-merge branch X' suggestion must be validated against why X failed review in the first place.
title: Completeness critic's remediation advice would have shipped a known bug; orchestrator overrode it mid-gate

evidence: One task's steps were internally inconsistent: an early step mandated inserting verbatim prose that itself contained a literal token, and a later verify step required a grep for that same token to return no matches. The two cannot both hold as written. The implementer faithfully inserted the prescribed text, so the verify-grep matched; the reviewer rated it clean but had to write a long judgment-call note reconciling the contradiction by appealing to substantive intent. The plan compiled and launched with no flag on the contradiction.
implication: This is a planning-authorship defect that survives to execution because nothing checks a task's verify-grep assertions against the text the same task inserts. A lightweight ultraplan/marker lint could catch 'verify asserts absence of a string this task's own body inserts' and surface it before launch as a conflict.
title: Self-contradictory task (insert text containing token X; then assert token X absent) passed review as clean with a judgment note

evidence: A plan requirement ('apply this reordering everywhere a certain event is handled') was satisfied in only the one handler the task's named file happened to contain, while two structurally-identical handlers in other locations were left unfixed — recreating the exact failure mode the requirement targeted. Because each task is scoped to its own file set, no per-task reviewer could see the siblings; only the cross-tree completeness critic caught it as a partial.
implication: When a plan requirement is cross-cutting ('every occurrence of pattern P'), splitting it across file-scoped tasks loses the global invariant. ultraplan guidance could flag requirements phrased as 'every/all occurrences' that span multiple task file-sets, and the completeness critic prompt should explicitly hunt for partially-applied cross-file invariants.
title: Cross-file plan requirement only partially satisfied (one of N sibling handlers fixed); per-task scope blindness

evidence: One task entered the fix loop, hit the 2-iteration cap, and failed — the underlying defect was a one-line wrong-API-name call. The salvage required a full additional resumed wave with adversarial review to fix what was a trivial substitution. Audit shows the heaviest cost in the run concentrated on a few high-turn implementer agents (one at ~70 turns / ~18K output tokens), and the misrank candidates list flagged a top-tier implementer running far longer than its peers — effort that did not prevent the wrong-API miss.
implication: A fix-loop that exhausts on a one-line error suggests the fix prompt is not carrying the reviewer's exact diagnosis sharply enough to land the trivial correction. Carrying the reviewer's precise 'use X not Y' as a structured prior-attempt note (which the salvage relaunch did do successfully) should be the default inside the fix loop, not only on operator-driven relaunch.
title: Fix-loop cap (2 iterations) exhausted on a single-line correction; the failure was a wrong-API mistake a sharper prompt could have averted

evidence: After the gate, the operator said 'proceed' as standing authorization. The orchestrator logged it but, on checking run state, found the salvage run's worktrees still active and unmerged, and explicitly declined to push/merge because doing so would ship the two known-open gaps. It restated exactly what it would do on completion and the single condition (gate comes back red) that would make it stop and surface instead. A clean example of the design's 'attention moves to the gate, not the mechanics' thesis holding under a blanket go-ahead.
implication: Positive exemplar — the standing-authorization-but-verify-state behavior is what the design wants. Worth preserving as a reference pattern; no change needed.
title: Operator granted standing authorization and the orchestrator correctly refused to push while the salvage run was still in flight

evidence: An 8-task parallel run (7-wide wave then a single dependent consolidation task) landed every task clean on the first pass.
