The standout: the completeness critic, facing the specific risk that the consolidation task's many cross-file anchor links might not resolve, independently authored a slug-resolver script and machine-verified that every cross-file and in-page anchor across all touched files resolves to a real header — going beyond the prescribed grep checks. The design did not ask for a custom verifier; the agent built the strongest available check for the actual risk.
implication: This emergent 'build the verifier the risk demands' behavior is exactly the verification-first ideal. Consider naming it in the completeness-critic prompt as a sanctioned move (write a deterministic resolver when link/anchor integrity is the risk) so it recurs reliably rather than by luck.
title: Clean 8-task / 2-wave run where the completeness critic wrote its own anchor-resolver to machine-verify every cross-reference

evidence: Although all reviews passed clean, the run surfaced a handful of real illustrative-snippet inconsistencies as advisory notes. The orchestrator presented a recommendation (polish the substantive few, leave the cosmetic ones) and the operator answered via a structured question/answer flow choosing 'polish then merge' and naming the landing branch. Attention stayed on the small set of judgment calls, exactly where the design intends.
implication: Positive exemplar of the gate doing its job. No change; reinforces that surfacing a short ranked advisory list (substantive vs cosmetic) at the gate is the right report shape.
title: Substantive review nits were resolved by structured operator questions, not a free-form gate stall

evidence: While inspecting the compiler's output, the operator's ad-hoc script assumed each entry in one of the compiler's list fields was an object with attribute access, but the entries were plain strings — producing a traceback ('str has no attribute get') even though the compile itself succeeded and the waves printed correctly. The same class of shape-mismatch traceback appears in a sibling run in this project, suggesting the compiler's output structure (which fields are lists-of-ids vs lists-of-objects) is non-obvious and easy to mis-handle.
implication: The compiler's emitted JSON should document, in-band, which fields are arrays of id-strings versus arrays of task objects — or the SKILL inspection guidance should show the canonical access pattern — so orchestrators stop writing fragile inspectors that throw on the real shape. A tiny schema note in the compiler README or its --help would remove a recurring stumble.
title: Operator's own inspection of compiler JSON threw an AttributeError on the output shape (recurs across 2 runs in this project)

evidence: The run began by invoking the engine with a bare plan-path string as its argument, but the committed workflow validates for a compiled wave array (or a path to an emitted bodies file) and fails loud on a plain string. The orchestrator had to stop, read the real skill procedure, run the compiler with the emit-launch step, derive the per-task knobs, and relaunch with the correct args object. The failed first launch spawned no agents (so nothing to clean up), but it was a full false start. This bundle's metadata also records planningFound:false despite a genuine, well-formed plan existing on disk.
implication: A common invocation shortcut ('run the workflow on this plan path') routes around the mandatory compile step and dead-ends on the args validator. Two fixes: (1) the launch validator's error message should explicitly name the compile-then-launch path as the remedy (it partly does); (2) investigate why the harvester recorded planningFound:false here — if planning detection keys off something the compile-skipping path omits, the harvest signal is being lost for exactly the runs that fumbled the launch.
title: First launch attempt failed: a plan-path string was passed where compiled waves were required (planningFound:false)

evidence: After a successful 11-task run, more than a dozen per-task worktree checkouts remained under the project's tool directory, each a full embedded git repo. When the operator prepared to commit unrelated local config, a dry-run add revealed git would have ingested all 13+ embedded repositories (git emitted 'adding embedded git repository' warnings for each) plus OS-junk files and machine-installed harness copies. The operator stopped before committing, then manually removed 13 leftover worktrees; even afterward a stray worktree directory and junk files lingered. Committing wholesale would have polluted the repo with run debris and nested repos.
implication: Worktree sweeping must be a guaranteed, run-terminal step the engine performs (or the gate enforces), not a manual operator afterthought — leftover worktrees are both a disk/debris problem and a real risk of getting committed as embedded repos. Additionally, the tool directory that holds worktrees and machine-installed harness copies should be gitignored by the install/hook so a careless add cannot pull them in.
title: Post-run worktrees not swept; repo nearly committed 13+ embedded git repositories as run debris; operator caught it at the gate

evidence: The plan compiled into one wide parallel wave of ~10 tasks writing disjoint files, plus a single trailing task gated by one marker edge onto an earlier task whose contract it consumed. No marker conflicts, no degrade, all tasks merged clean with zero fix iterations. Routing to the parallel engine was clearly the right call and the Type/Depends-on markers produced exactly the intended shape — a textbook well-marked plan.
implication: Positive routing exemplar: disjoint-file fan-out with a single dependency edge is the engine's sweet spot. No change. Useful as a calibration baseline for what a healthy marked plan looks like versus the contradiction/partial-application defects seen in the larger sibling run.
title: Wide-then-narrow wave shape (10 disjoint-file tasks, then 1 dependent) parallelized correctly with a single marker edge

evidence: At the pre-merge gate the two non-blocking findings were each a one-line internal-consistency gap traceable to the plan being under-specified: a doc row prescribed by the plan contradicted that file's own guidance, and a count was fixed in a headline while the inventory list it summarized was not updated. The agents flagged both but correctly declined to auto-fix, since fixing would diverge from the approved plan. The work itself was clean (0 fix iterations across all tasks); the residue was authored-plan imprecision.
implication: Recurring theme across this project's runs: the residual gaps at the gate are dominated by plan-authorship imprecision (self-contradiction, partial cross-file invariants, stale summaries), not implementation defects. Investment in pre-launch plan linting (ultraplan) likely yields more gate-quality improvement than tuning the implementer/reviewer loop.
title: Two operator-flagged advisory gaps at the gate were both plan under-specification, not implementer error

evidence: Acceptance bound to the committed unit suite (mode=suite); the suite deliberately excluded all UI/runtime/persistence code paths, and the completeness critic explicitly named those paths as the only untested ones 'by design'. The pre-merge report showed every gate green (all tasks complete, full suite passing, all wave merges intact). Yet the very first manual launch of the built artifact threw a recurring exception on a periodic-autosave path that fired before initialization completed — a defect zero of the green checks could have caught. The clean gate and a shipping-blocking runtime bug were simultaneously true.
implication: When acceptance=suite AND the completeness critic reports that whole runtime/UI/persistence layers are untested by design, the gate report should escalate that to a first-class caveat (not buried under 'not unit-tested'), and the engine should consider requiring a minimal smoke/boot check before declaring the gate green — a 'green suite' is not 'the thing runs'.
title: All-green suite-acceptance gate coexisted with a real boot-time runtime defect the suite could not reach

evidence: After the engine finished and produced a clean gate report, the orchestrator (outside the engine's wave/worktree machinery) hand-fixed the boot-time defect plus stale cache-version tokens directly on the integration branch. Multiple files were modified but left UNCOMMITTED, described as 'fold them into the merge commit when you decide at the gate'. The run's own completeness verification had already run against a HEAD that did not include these fixes, so the verified SHA and the actual working tree diverged, and the engine no longer governs the changes.
implication: The engine should define what happens to repairs discovered AFTER the gate report but BEFORE the merge decision: either forbid out-of-band edits to the integration branch, or require they re-trigger verification, so the 'verified HEAD == gate report' invariant the report asserts cannot silently break between report and merge.
title: Orchestrator's post-run repair edits left uncommitted and dangling on the integration branch at the gate

evidence: Roughly two-thirds of the run's tasks all wrote the same monolithic orchestrator file. The compiler's write-after-write rule correctly chained every one of them into a single forced line, so only the small minority of tasks that lived in extracted pure modules fanned out (a front of about three waves, widest ~4). The remainder marched one task per wave down a long diagonal. The orchestrator itself noted the worktree+independent-review+merge ceremony was 'pure overhead on the serial portion' with no parallel work to hide it behind, and that wall-clock savings were modest (≈18 effective steps vs the task count).
implication: The wave-plan transparency render (and the routing recommendation) should surface a 'parallel-payoff' signal — e.g. fraction of tasks on the longest single-file chain vs total — so the operator sees up front when most of the DAG is a serial spine and the full worktree ceremony buys little over the sequential fallback.
title: Single-file integration trunk forced a long serial write-after-write spine; parallel fan-out was a thin fringe

evidence: When the operator questioned the mostly-serial execution shape mid-run, the orchestrator conceded the routing was correct 'by the letter' of the fit rule (enough tasks AND a wave with parallel work) but 'borderline by the spirit' (does parallel speedup clear the overhead?), and that the sequential per-task fallback would have been an entirely defensible, possibly leaner choice. The decisive tie-breaker that would have justified the parallel engine — a held-out-exam acceptance override — had been explicitly opted out of when acceptance was set to the suite mode.
implication: The fit rule that recommends the parallel engine should incorporate a parallel-payoff threshold (not just task-count AND any-parallel-wave). When the held-out-exam risk override is absent and the DAG is dominated by one serial file chain, the recommendation should at least flag that the sequential fallback is competitive rather than recommending the heavier engine unconditionally.
title: Engine self-assessed its own routing as borderline; by-the-spirit the sequential fallback would have been leaner

evidence: About 40% of the tasks were originally written as deliberately lower-detail roadmap outlines explicitly marked 'not part of the immediate run — expand into full test-first detail once predecessors land' (a YAGNI/just-in-time stance the planning agent argued for). A first scoped compile correctly narrowed the run to only the execution-ready tasks. The operator then reversed that ('do it all'), the outline tasks were expanded and folded back in, and the full set was launched — and the run completed with the whole expanded set passing review and merging, including the parts that had been judged speculative to detail in advance.
implication: The engine tolerated executing tasks the plan itself rated as under-specified, which both validates resilience and risks washing out the planning agent's deliberate detail-deferral signal. Consider having the compiler/transparency render mark tasks that carry an explicit 'not-yet-execution-ready / expand-later' annotation, so the operator re-expanding scope sees which tasks were promoted from outline status.
title: Tasks authored as deferred 'expand-later' roadmap outlines were re-expanded and executed in the same run, and still succeeded

evidence: The engine presented its one human gate as a clean, all-green pre-merge report and an explicit three-way decision. The operator answered the gate question not by deciding but by requesting to run the built artifact locally to play with it. The session then flowed into manual playtesting and onward into authoring a brand-new follow-on design task. The run's integration branch was never merged within the captured arc; it was still sitting at the gate, unmerged, alongside the uncommitted repair edits.
implication: The design intends the gate to be where the operator spends attention and reaches a decision; here their attention went to exploration and a new task instead, and the completed work was left dangling. The engine could treat 'play/inspect the artifact' as a recognized pre-decision gate state with a persistent reminder that a merge decision is still owed, so a clean run does not silently end un-landed.
title: At the single human gate the operator declined the Approve/Salvage/Redirect decision and pivoted to playtest, leaving the run stranded unmerged

evidence: Because the testable surface was an internal module API whose behavior/data-shapes the spec fixed but whose names it deliberately left to implementation, the held-out-exam acceptance mode risked failing purely on naming. The orchestrator surfaced this as an operator fork (held-out exam vs committed-suite acceptance) rather than deciding silently, requiring an operator answer. Separately, a configuration step crashed with a type error (a string indexed as if it were a structured object) during acceptance setup, requiring a retry. Both ate pre-launch turns.
implication: When the testable surface is an internal API where the spec intentionally leaves names open, the sealing tooling should detect that 'names-left-to-implementation' shape and steer toward suite-acceptance automatically (or warn) rather than surfacing a fork; and the config crash points at a fragility in how the acceptance object is parsed that deserves a guard.
title: Acceptance-mode fork plus a tooling crash mid-configuration consumed planning attention before launch

evidence: Several honest/self-limiting behaviors appeared without the design forcing them: the planning agent refused to silently choose an acceptance mode and instead escalated a fork it judged the operator's to own; one task agent deliberately withheld importing a symbol produced by a not-yet-merged upstream task so its own wave's tests would stay green, then reconciled after the upstream merged (logged as a judgment call); and the orchestrator volunteered that its own routing recommendation was borderline rather than defending it. The completeness critic also distinguished genuinely-met behavioral requirements from harmless dead-code leftovers instead of flagging cosmetics as gaps.
implication: These are positive exemplars of agents respecting boundaries and surfacing uncertainty. Worth capturing as calibration anchors for the reading lenses (the 'declined to do X because Y was impossible/inappropriate' pattern) and for the reviewer prompts, which correctly separated met-requirement-with-dead-leftover from a real gap.
title: Self-correcting agent behaviors: declined to decide a reserved fork silently, held a cross-module import to keep its wave green, flagged its own routing

evidence: On a large multi-task feature plan, the engine's launch contract required every task's full verbatim body passed inline in one tool-call argument. The compiler emitted task structure but not bodies, so the orchestrator had to re-extract them; the combined bodies were far too large to emit as one escaped value reliably. The orchestrator worked around it by reducing each body to a short pointer into the plan file present in each worktree — a deviation from the self-contained-body contract that silently breaks when the base branch lacks the plan.
implication: Make the compiler the single source of truth and add a file-backed body-delivery path so the orchestrator passes a path, not tens of KB inline; keep inline delivery for tiny plans. (Confirmed adopted in a later run.)
title: Verbatim task bodies do not fit inline in the launch call; orchestrator improvised a plan-pointer workaround

evidence: Every task worktree was a clean checkout with no virtualenv and no installed package deps for either of the repo's two language stacks. Implementers fell back from the plan's declared test command to a generic invocation, hit repeated module-not-found failures until they ran an install by hand, and one task created a symlink hack into the main repo to satisfy an import. A single run-wide test command could not cover both stacks, so the integration test-pass signal was computed inconsistently.
implication: Add an explicit per-worktree bootstrap/install knob and per-task test commands, plus polyglot/monorepo guidance, so a worktree can run the plan's own test steps as written without the agent improvising environment fixes. (Confirmed adopted in a later run as a bootstrap command knob.)
title: Fresh task worktrees lack language toolchains; polyglot test commands fail until agents improvise installs

evidence: At the pre-merge gate the held-out acceptance runner failed at test COLLECTION (a repo library could not be imported in the runner's detached worktree), which surfaced as acceptance-failed even though the contracts it covered passed inside the main integration test run. The gate-time runner context differed from the context in which the exam author had proven the suite red, so the official runner could not actually execute the exam, and the operator had to manually distinguish a false-red from a real red.
implication: Make the gate runner reproduce the target repo's own test bootstrap, require the seal author to prove RED through the exact gate runner path so an un-importable suite is caught at seal time, and distinguish a collection/environment error from a genuine assertion failure in the runner output. (Later runs show a redKind assertion/collection distinction and a bootstrap-error status were added.)
title: Sealed acceptance exam false-red: collection/import error in the gate runner reported as a feature failure

evidence: All tasks completed, merged, and the primary suites were green, yet the completeness critic independently found that the client posted uploads under one field key while the server read a different key, so the entire headline drop-to-stage-to-approve flow silently processed zero documents — and no test exercised that upload endpoint. It also caught broken provenance (raw source never persisted, references pointing at an ephemeral gitignored path). A targeted corrective wave fixed both and went green. The integration-level critic surfaced exactly the class of defect green unit suites cannot.
implication: Reinforces the value of an integration-level completeness critic distinct from per-task review; consider prompting it specifically for cross-component wiring mismatches (client/server contract keys, declared-vs-read field names) since those slip past disjoint per-file TDD.
title: Adversarial review caught a headline-feature dead bug (field-name mismatch) that all unit tests missed

evidence: While the parallel build ran headless for over an hour, the operator's attention went to a real-world external prerequisite (a deploy credential turned out to be read-only, blocking the feature's live path) and to provisioning a scoped write credential — surfaced proactively by the assistant early ('good thing we checked'). The operator never touched implementation; the one human gate was the pre-merge review. This is the intended attention shift (plan/verify, not implement) playing out cleanly.
implication: Positive exemplar: the autonomous-build-plus-single-gate posture freed the operator for the genuinely human-only blocker. No change needed; useful as a calibration reference for the design's thesis.
title: Operator spent the autonomous stretch on an external credential prerequisite, not on the build — design intent realized

evidence: A large feature plan fanned out across many parallel worktree agents (impl + per-task reviewers + per-wave merges) over roughly an hour and a half. Of all implementation tasks, only one needed a fix iteration; the rest reviewed clean first pass. Two heavy implementation agents were flagged as tier-misrank candidates (high turn/token counts on tasks assigned a lower tier), feeding the next plan's tier assignments.
implication: The misrank-candidate signal is working as a feed-forward; consider surfacing it more prominently so operators actually re-tier recurring heavy task shapes (e.g. the largest module in a plan) on the next run.
title: Large plan ran ~46 agents / millions of tokens / ~90 min and merged clean with only one fix iteration

evidence: After the pre-merge gate found real defects, the operator chose the corrective-redirect path: a single parallel wave of three narrowly-scoped fix tasks onto the same integration branch, each clean with no fix iterations, taking the run green on re-gate. The acceptance exam was waived with the recorded false-red reason. The redirect mechanism turned a non-approvable run into an approvable one without re-running the whole plan.
implication: Positive exemplar for the deterministic redirect path; the small targeted corrective wave is the right cost/benefit shape after a gate rejection. No change needed.
title: Redirect (corrective wave) is cheap and surgical: 3 fix tasks, 0 fix iterations, straight to green

evidence: The plan's acceptance line carried a 'pending seal' placeholder that the compiler treated as neither sealed nor waived, so it refused to launch and surfaced the choice to the operator (seal an exam now vs waive and rely on the test gate). The operator chose to waive. This is the one compile failure the engine deliberately escalates rather than resolving, but the placeholder is a common authoring state that interrupts an otherwise autonomous launch.
implication: Consider having the plan-authoring skill refuse to emit a 'pending'/placeholder acceptance line (force seal-or-waive at authoring time), or have the compiler give a sharper 'placeholder detected — seal or waive' message so the interruption is anticipated rather than a surprise at launch.
title: Unsealed Acceptance placeholder forced a mid-flow operator decision the engine refused to make

evidence: On a fully-clean run (all tasks clean, suite green), the completeness critic explicitly named the feature's highest-risk premise — an identity-equality assumption between two external-API endpoints that NO automated test can prove — and flagged it, along with several untested branches that gate every request, as deferred-to-manual-verification by design rather than asserting the feature was proven live. It distinguished 'code-complete' from 'proven live' and pushed the unverifiable claim into the operator's post-merge runbook.
implication: Strong positive exemplar of self-limiting/honest behavior (akin to the planning-agent-declining calibration). Worth encoding as a critic expectation: explicitly separate 'verified by suite' from 'code-ready but requires manual/live confirmation' and route the latter to the runbook.
title: Completeness critic correctly demoted the single highest-risk assumption to the manual runbook instead of false-passing it

evidence: The per-worktree dependency-install command for one stack failed because the worktree had an old package-manager version lacking modern editable-install support, and a separate suite test needed a newer language runtime than the worktree provided — surfaced as judgment calls. Implementers correctly judged their own task had no dependency on that stack and proceeded, and a pre-existing cross-suite test flaked under the same root cause. The bootstrap failure was tolerated rather than blocking, but it left the environment partially unprepared.
implication: When the bootstrap command fails, surface it distinctly (it is neither a task failure nor fully benign) and consider verifying the worktree's toolchain versions meet the repo's declared minimums before dispatching, so a stale package manager doesn't silently degrade the environment.
title: Per-worktree bootstrap silently failed on a stale package manager / language-version mismatch; agents proceeded anyway

evidence: A well-marked plan compiled to a wide first wave of independent tasks, then two single-task waves for the route handlers and the server wiring that genuinely depended on the earlier modules. Every task merged in order with no fix iterations and no conflicts; the only marker_conflict was an informational non-path Files token and two informational prose-reference inferences that changed no ordering. The routing recommendation (parallel) matched the actual execution.
implication: Positive routing exemplar; the marker contract + disjoint-write inference produced exactly the right wave shape. No change needed.
title: Clean wave shape on a well-marked plan: disjoint-write parallelism plus a short serialized tail matched how the run went

evidence: The held-out acceptance suite for this stack ran and all of its tests passed, but the gate runner's anti-false-green guard verifies 'tests actually ran' via a marker that only the pytest framework writes; the other test framework never writes it, so the runner refused to certify and exited non-zero ('exited 0 but ran no sealed tests — refusing to false-green'). The exam substantively passed yet was uncertifiable by the official runner, and the operator had to override the artifact to approve.
implication: The false-green guard is framework-specific and silently neutralizes the acceptance gate for any non-pytest suite. Make the 'tests actually ran' check framework-agnostic (parse the runner's own pass/skip counts per framework) so a passing acceptance suite in a JS/other-runtime stack is certifiable without an operator override.
title: Sealed exam runner cannot certify a non-pytest suite — passing acceptance tests returned a blocking non-zero exit

evidence: After a small, clean ultrapowers fix landed, the standard deploy path (pull the default branch on the prod box) would have pushed the entire long-lived feature branch — dozens of commits and thousands of lines including unfinished, write-capable, not-yet-ready functionality — to production. The assistant caught the scope mismatch and refused to deploy on a bare 'deploy' instruction, surfacing it to the operator. The ultrapowers run itself was fine; the hazard was at the post-merge boundary between the integration branch and the operator's release ritual.
implication: The post-merge runbook handoff should explicitly flag when the integration base branch is far ahead of the deploy target (commit/line delta), so the operator is warned before a release ritual ships more than the run produced. Belongs in the finishing/handoff guidance.
title: Deploy-scope trap: a tiny approved fix sat on a long feature branch that the deploy path would push wholesale to prod

evidence: In a small 4-task run where peer tasks finished in tens of turns, one task assigned the middle tier ran ~140 turns and the most output tokens by a wide margin — flagged as the run's lone tier-misrank candidate. The transcript ties the cost to a justified but expensive deviation: the implementer reworked a dependency-injection seam to dodge a cross-worker test-mock-leakage quirk in the test runner rather than use the plan's literal approach. The reviewer and critic judged it correct.
implication: Test-runner isolation quirks (shared-process mock leakage under parallel suites) can inflate an otherwise small task's cost beyond its tier. The misrank signal correctly caught it; consider feeding 'task touched shared test-harness/mocking infrastructure' as a tier-bump heuristic at compile time.
title: One standard-tier task ran ~6x the turns of its peers — a tier-misrank candidate driven by a test-isolation deviation

evidence: The independent acceptance author, working from the spec alone, deliberately designed the held-out exam to exercise the durability contract at the HTTP boundary — booting the server, seeding on-disk state to prove boot-time rehydration, and hitting the list/discard endpoints — explicitly avoiding the paid external-LLM upload path. It proved the suite red at baseline for honest feature-absence reasons. This is unprompted cost-and-side-effect-aware test design beyond what the sealing brief mandated.
implication: Positive frontier exemplar (cost-aware, side-effect-avoiding exam authoring). Worth capturing in the seal-author guidance as an encouraged pattern: prefer the cheapest deterministic boundary that still proves the contract, and avoid paid/external side-effect paths in held-out exams.
title: Seal author proactively chose an HTTP-boundary exam to avoid a paid external code path

evidence: An earlier run's committed write-side harness mutated its exported meta object at runtime; the engine parsed that export as a static literal and did not expose it as a runtime binding, so the script threw a reference error at load and spawned zero agents. The operator's recourse was to hand-author an equivalent ad-hoc workflow file and launch that instead — which ran the same waves successfully. This is exactly the runtime-nondeterminism the committed-harness design exists to remove, reached because the committed harness itself was broken on that engine version.
implication: A zero-agent engine preflight that exercises the exact meta-exposure path BEFORE the real launch is the right guard (later runs show such a probe returning a meta-exposure flag, confirming the static-meta fix). Keep the probe pinned to the same parse path the real harness uses so an engine-side parse change is caught before a real run.
title: Historical: committed harness crashed at launch on runtime meta mutation; operator fell back to a hand-authored ad-hoc workflow

evidence: Because this run executed via a hand-authored ad-hoc workflow rather than the committed registry harness, the run's effort-audit recorded all ~29 agents with role 'unknown' and could surface no tier-misrank candidates — the audit parses role markers that only the committed harness emits. The transcripts and token counts exist, but the per-role attribution that drives tier feedback is absent.
implication: The harvester/audit should detect and label runs that did not use the committed harness (ad-hoc script path) so their unknown-role audit is understood as a tooling limit, not a data gap — and so cross-run cost comparisons don't treat them as comparable to harness runs.
title: Effort audit blind to ad-hoc/legacy harnesses: 29 agents all logged with unknown role

evidence: The final integration review walked a 12-item checklist citing exact file:line evidence for each, and explicitly discharged the cannot-verify-from-diff items by executing the integrated tree end-to-end (booting the server and exercising the wired behavior) rather than inferring from the diff. It separately and honestly enumerated the surfaces left unverified by the suite (lifecycle glue, UI smoke) as gaps-by-design rather than claiming full coverage.
implication: Positive exemplar: the completeness critic running behavior on the integrated tree (not diff-reading) is what catches wiring bugs. Reinforces prompting the critic to execute, not just read, and to label by-design coverage gaps distinctly from defects.
title: Integration critic discharged every 'cannot-verify' item by running the integrated tree, not by reading diffs

evidence: This bundle is not an ultrapowers wave-execution run at all: it is a research/Q&A + brainstorming + sequential-phase session that used several HAND-AUTHORED, ad-hoc Dynamic Workflows (a research-grounding workflow and three temp-file phase-execution scripts) plus a deep-research-style multi-agent answer. The harvester picked it up because it shares the per-run subagents/workflows transcript directory shape; planning-found is false and there is no committed-harness launch, no wave plan, no integration branch, and no pre-merge gate.
implication: Harvesting on transcript-directory shape alone over-collects: any use of the raw Workflow tool (research, brainstorm-grounding, ad-hoc phase execution) is misclassified as an ultrapowers run. The harvester should require a committed-harness signal (the registry meta.name / a wave-plan or integration-branch artifact) before counting a session as an ultrapowers run.
title: Harvester surfaced a non-ultrapowers session as an ultrapowers run (workflow-transcript shape false positive)

evidence: Across one session the operator parallelized non-implementation work via the raw Workflow tool: a multi-agent grounding pass to map the repo and verify platform capabilities before brainstorming, then a parallel plan-drafting workflow that locked a shared spine and wrote detailed per-phase tasks concurrently, then sequential phase-execution scripts. This is parallelism applied to the planning/research half of the lifecycle — the half the committed engine deliberately leaves human-driven — and it ran as ad-hoc scripts because no sanctioned read-only harness covers it.
implication: There is real demand for a sanctioned read-only parallel harness for the pre-implementation phases (research-grounding, parallel plan-drafting) that today is met by ad-hoc scripts. The read/write boundary already permits improvised read-only workflows; consider promoting a frozen read-only grounding/plan-drafting harness so this common pattern isn't reinvented per session.
title: Operator reached for ad-hoc parallel workflows to fan out brainstorming and plan-drafting, not just code execution

evidence: The bundle's effort audit records three agents and thousands of output tokens, all role 'unknown', for what is actually a research/brainstorm session's ad-hoc subagents — not ultrapowers execution. Counting this token spend as ultrapowers cost pollutes any cross-run cost or parallelism-payoff analysis the ledger would compute.
implication: Tie cost attribution to the harvester-classification fix: cost from ad-hoc/non-ultrapowers sessions must be excluded from (or clearly segregated in) any cross-run cost rollup, or parallelism-payoff metrics will be skewed by sessions that never ran a wave.
title: Audit attributed real cost (3 agents, ~13.5k output tokens) to a session that did no ultrapowers work

evidence: In a multi-wave run, one implementation task failed with status 'failed' / review 'agent-error' from a transient infrastructure overload error during the implementer (recorded as a judgment call: a null-deref on the agent's own status object). Because nothing in the DAG depended on that task, every other wave completed, the integration tree merged, and the full test suite passed green. The completeness critic — not any per-task review — was the only thing that caught that an entire implementation task (a security-relevant, gated-write half of the feature) was wholly absent from the integration: no source, no tests, no registration, no commit. The green test gate was misleading because the missing task had no tests in the tree to fail.
implication: A transient (non-deterministic, retryable) implementer failure is silently survivable when no downstream task depends on it: the run reports tests.passed:true and only the completeness critic flags the hole. The engine should distinguish a transient/infra failure (auto-retry candidate, like the operator's manual Salvage) from a genuine red, and should surface a hard banner at the gate when ANY implementation task is 'failed' even if tests pass — the test-green signal must not visually dominate a missing-task signal.
title: Transient model-overload error killed a mid-DAG implementation task; gate stayed green but feature was half-built

evidence: The failed task and a later cleanup task were siblings with a dependency edge through intermediate waves but no direct edge to each other. The cleanup task, executing in its own worktree with only its own body, correctly did its job (added a user-facing suggestion advertising a capability the plan said would exist) — but because the capability's implementing task had transiently failed, the suggestion now pointed at a nonexistent feature. The completeness critic flagged this as exactly the dishonest-affordance regression the cleanup task existed to prevent. Worktree isolation means no task agent can know a sibling failed; the failure only became visible at integration.
implication: Worktree-pure isolation has a blind spot: a task that legitimately depends on the OUTCOME of a sibling (not its files) cannot detect that the sibling failed, so it can ship work premised on a capability that never arrived. When a task fails, its transitive 'advertises/assumes' siblings should be flagged for re-review or cascade-blocked, not just its file-dependency descendants — the completeness critic catching it is the safety net, but a cheaper signal upstream would help.
title: A later-wave sibling task introduced an affordance pointing at the failed task's missing capability — a self-inflicted honesty regression

evidence: Faced with one 'failed' implementation task whose failure was transient (an infra overload, not a code/design defect) and whose files were untouched by any merged task, the operator recognized a textbook Salvage: relaunch only that task onto the same integration branch with the body unchanged. It applied cleanly, passed adversarial review with no functional defects, and the full suite advanced green. The two findings (missing task + the dishonest affordance pointing at it) resolved together because rebuilding the capability made the affordance honest again.
implication: Positive exemplar: the Salvage path is well-shaped for the common single-transient-failure case. Worth noting that the operator had to manually reason that the failure was transient and the files were uncontended — the engine could pre-compute and recommend exactly this Salvage scope (it already knows the failed task, its edges, and which files other tasks touched).
title: Salvage worked exactly as designed — operator re-ran only the single transiently-failed task and it merged clean

evidence: The audit block flagged two misrank candidates. The single most expensive agent was a most-capable-tier implementer running far more turns and output tokens than the next-largest agent (roughly double the turns of the median heavy agent), and a cheap-tier implementer ran a surprisingly high turn count for its tier. The run's totals were dominated by a long tail of reviewers (every implementation task drew one or two opus reviewers, several at high turn counts), so review — not implementation — was the bulk of the spend across this run.
implication: Per-task tier assignment under-predicted the actual effort of the heaviest implementation task and over/under-predicted a cheap one; feed these misrank candidates into the next plan's tier assignment as the audit step intends. Separately, the dominant cost center is reviewer fan-out (adversarial = two opus passes on many tasks): the cost lens should weigh whether every high-surface task truly needs adversarial review, since reviewers collectively outweigh implementers here.
title: Tier-misrank: a most-capable implementer burned ~2x the turns/tokens of any other agent while a cheap-tier task on the same wave-graph stayed small

evidence: Acceptance was waived in favor of in-plan TDD + a full-suite gate, on the rationale that the deterministic core was fixture-tested and the live external-integration path depended on operator-provisioned credentials a worktree exam could not exercise. The completeness critic then surfaced that a repeatedly-invoked type-check gate step in the plan was a no-op (the repo had no type-check config and the runtime doesn't type-check), so the plan's 'no type errors' acceptance claim was vacuous; and that the live read/write behavior and the gated-write approval path had no automated coverage at all — verification rode entirely on a manual owner task performed after merge.
implication: A waiver justified by 'the committed suite covers it' is only as strong as the committed suite — here a gate step that silently did nothing inflated the apparent coverage. The compiler/gate should detect and warn when a plan's named gate command is a no-op on this repo (e.g. a type-check with no config), rather than carrying it into the transparency render as a real gate. Waivers that defer a whole capability to a manual post-merge task should be surfaced more loudly at the gate.
title: Waived held-out exam left the entire agent/live-integration half verified only by a manual owner task and a vacuous type-check gate

evidence: After the parallel run merged, a substantial sequential tail followed entirely outside the engine: several fixes to make the live external-data read path actually work (wrong credential variable, wrong query API/scope), a new local-dev auth-bypass flag so the app could be run without its production proxy, and then visual iteration on the UI. The dev flag, once written into the repo's env file, broke roughly nine security/auth-gate tests because the test runner auto-loads that env file, so the bypass activated during the suite; the fix moved the flag to a run-command rather than the env file. None of this was waved or engine-driven — it was the operator hand-driving verification the waiver had deferred.
implication: The verification the waiver pushed to 'after merge' turned into a long manual tail with its own regressions (a documented dev flag that is a footgun when placed where the test runner reads env). This is the predictable cost of waiving the held-out exam on an externally-integrated feature: the real integration risk surfaces post-merge, by hand, unparallelized. Argues for either a sealed exam that exercises the boundary with fixtures, or an explicit gate warning that the live path is unverified at merge time.
title: Long post-merge tail of live-integration and local-dev fixes, including a dev-bypass flag that broke auth-gate tests via env auto-load

evidence: A small plan (three implementation tasks + a gate) compiled to two waves: two independent disjoint-write tasks in parallel, then one task depending on a single marker edge, with the gate compiled into run config. All tasks merged with clean reviews and zero fix iterations; the suite went from baseline to a few more tests, all green. The markers were well-authored — exactly one inferred dependency edge, no marker conflicts, mode parallel. The routing recommendation (ultrapowers, parallel) matched how the run actually went.
implication: Positive exemplar of well-marked small-plan parallelization: disjoint writes stayed parallel, the lone real dependency serialized correctly, adversarial review was applied only to the one security-relevant task while routine tasks stayed lean. This is the target behavior; nothing to change.
title: Clean small parallel run: disjoint-write tasks fanned out, single dependent task in wave 2, zero fixes, gate green

evidence: An early compile invocation exited non-zero with a Python FileNotFoundError traceback because the plan path was passed as a repo-relative path but resolved against a working directory where that relative path did not exist. The operator recovered on a subsequent attempt (the compiler output then arrived normally), so it was a transient stumble rather than a blocker — but the failure surfaced as a raw stack trace rather than an actionable message.
implication: When the plan path does not resolve, the compiler emits a bare FileNotFoundError traceback instead of a clear 'plan not found at <resolved abspath>; pass an absolute path or run from the repo root' message. A small ergonomics fix: resolve and echo the absolute path, and fail with a one-line actionable error rather than a traceback.
title: Compiler threw FileNotFoundError on a relative plan path resolved from the wrong working directory

evidence: The compiler's task-parse picked up a bare symbol name (not a file path) that appeared in one task's Files block, listing it alongside the real file path in both the task writes set and the launch_waves files. The orchestrator recognized it as a non-load-bearing stray token and dropped it from the launch args, surfacing the fact as an informational note in the transparency render rather than a conflict.
implication: Minor parser robustness: a Files block that mixes a real path with a prose symbol name lets a non-path token leak into the writes/launch sets. Harmless here because it was caught and dropped, but it would create a phantom write-contention edge if two tasks named the same symbol. The compiler could filter Files tokens that aren't path-shaped at parse time and report them as inference notes, which is roughly what happened by hand.
title: Compiler captured a stray non-path token from a Files block and correctly dropped it from launch args

evidence: Acceptance was waived on the basis that the change was largely visual and the verifiable logic was committed-test covered, with a manual visual check named as the residual verification. At the gate the completeness critic explicitly flagged that the rebuilt view had no automated coverage and that the manual visual check had not been performed (review agents are read-only). The assistant noted it would rather perform the visual check before merging, but the operator answered the gate question with 'Approve & merge now', so the run merged with the waiver's own named verification still outstanding.
implication: The waiver mechanism records a residual manual check, but nothing enforces or even re-confirms it at the gate beyond a critic note — the operator can (and did) approve past their own stated verification step. Not necessarily wrong (operator's prerogative), but the gate could make an unperformed waiver-named manual check a distinct, acknowledged line item rather than one bullet among the critic's findings.
title: Operator approved at the gate without performing the waiver's own manual visual check, knowingly

evidence: This run is an 11-iteration review-and-fix loop on ultrapowers' own code. The agent's own honest accounting (slice tail): 'your /goal requires "two consecutive iterations with zero P1s and no substantive P2s" before stopping, and its Stop hook blocks me from ending early. But three adversarial reviewers probing an input grammar will essentially ALWAYS find one more exotic variant — the condition as written may never mechanically terminate.' P1 trajectory across iterations was 6 → 2 → 1 → 1 → 0 → 0 → 0 → 0 → 1 → 0 → 0; iterations 5-11 surfaced only 'a Files entry missing its colon', 'depends on with a space not a hyphen', 'prototype-named task ids' at ~500K-1M subagent tokens each. The operator hit a spend limit during this stretch and ultimately had to interrupt twice ([Request interrupted by user]) to force a stop and release.
implication: A termination condition phrased as 'N consecutive clean rounds' is unsafe when the thing being reviewed is an open grammar/parser that adversarial reviewers can always find one more edge of. ultralearn/ultrapowers loop or goal harnesses should cap iterations, weight findings by realistic impact (so colon-less-Files-entry P2s don't reset the counter), and trust an agent's diminishing-returns judgment to end a loop rather than a Stop hook overriding it. Capture this as a named anti-pattern in the lenses doc.
title: A /goal Stop-hook turned a self-review loop practically non-terminating against an input-grammar; the agent kept grinding past the point of value because the harness blocked it from stopping

evidence: The dogfood cycles inside this run did NOT pause at the Step-3 human gate: the assistant launched waves with 'Wave plan (approved per the goal's standing authorization): wave 1 = tasks 1,3,5,9,10; wave 2 = ...'. The design intends the human to spend attention approving the wave-plan interpretation and the pre-merge report; instead the /goal hook pre-authorized those gates and the operator's only interventions were two hard interrupts to stop the loop and a terse 'skill ok / ALLGREEN' acknowledgement. The agent's closing note concedes 'Your instinct to call it was correct, and roughly two iterations earlier than I flagged it.'
implication: When a meta-loop pre-authorizes ultrapowers' gates, the engine's careful human-in-the-loop design is bypassed and the operator's attention migrates to babysitting the loop's stop condition — the exact opposite of where the design wants it. ultralearn should flag runs where standing/blanket gate authorization replaced the per-run human approval, and the routing/loop guidance should warn that auto-approval delegation defeats the gate it was built to protect.
title: Operator attention was spent on a runaway review loop, not at any ultrapowers gate — wave plans were auto-approved by 'the goal's standing authorization'

evidence: audit.totals for this bundle is 860 turns / 116,446 outputTokens across 29 agents (11 impl Sonnet, ~13 Opus reviewers, Haiku merge/setup) — but that captures ONE dogfood wave; the slice shows cycles 1-4 each ran their own /ultrapowers workflow (wf_6f360c44-a44, wf_ded70299-5aa preflight, etc.) and cycles 5-11 were inline, so the bundle's audit block under-counts the true 11-cycle cost. The agent states each late round cost ~500K-1M subagent tokens for marginal findings and that the operator hit a spend limit mid-stretch. misrankCandidates is empty (no tier-misrank flagged), and the per-cycle Opus-reviewer concentration (most-turns agents are all claude-opus-4-8 reviewers, 28-52 turns) shows review dominated spend, as designed.
implication: harvest_runs.py / audit_run.py attribute cost to a single workflow id, but a self-development meta-session fans cost across many nested workflow ids plus inline rounds — the bundle's audit.totals materially under-reports true run cost for these sessions. ultralearn cost-lens guidance should note this under-count for multi-launch sessions, and the cost lens should weight 'tokens-per-finding-of-realistic-impact' rather than raw output tokens.
title: Diminishing-returns iterations cost ~500K-1M subagent tokens each to surface increasingly exotic, low-impact findings — and the bundle audit only captured the final inline tail, not the per-cycle ultrapowers token spend

evidence: The early cycles (1-4, the high-value ones) found genuine correctness defects via the engine's own parallel review: compile_plan.py's sequential degrade emitted document order over declared dependencies; Test: paths silently dropped; the documented ambiguous-Files conservative default was unimplemented; a fenced 'git push' example silently reclassified a task as release and excluded it from execution; write-after-create guaranteed spurious cycle rejections of legal plans; one blocked wave spuriously cascade-failed whole runs. The test count grew 58 → 156. The agent: 'the dogfooding itself surfaced several bugs.' These were caught because three fresh-eyes reviewers (code/docs/interop) ran in parallel per cycle.
implication: Positive exemplar: parallel adversarial review at scale is an effective bug-finder on the engine's own grammar/compiler, and the verification-first thesis held. Worth recording as a calibration data point that the FIRST few review passes carry nearly all the value — reinforcing the 'cap iterations early' implication above.
title: Dogfooding ultrapowers on its own code surfaced real correctness bugs the test suite missed — including the compiler emitting document order over declared Depends-on edges

evidence: The plan path is docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-11-review-cycle-1-fixes.md — a self-development plan whose tasks ARE edits to ultrapowers' own files (compile_plan.py, workflow.js, plan-markers.md, reviewer-prompts.md). The slice's user turns are dominated by P1/P2/P3 audit reports and full file dumps of the repo's own source/tests/references; the actual wave-execution turns (Workflow launched, merge results) are a thin minority. This is the classic meta-session hazard: a session that DISCUSSES and edits ultrapowers rather than executing an unrelated feature plan, which inflates the slice with self-referential content the reading lenses can mistake for execution signal.
implication: harvest_runs.py / the reader rubric should detect home-origin self-development sessions (planPath under docs/superpowers/plans referencing the engine's own files; user turns that are P-tier audit reports) and tag them as meta-sessions so readers down-weight self-referential audit prose and focus on the execution events. Otherwise these runs flood the ledger with findings about ultrapowers-quoting-itself.
title: Meta-session: the harvested slice is almost entirely audit deliverables and pasted source files ABOUT ultrapowers, not a feature being built — low execution-signal density

evidence: Routing to /ultrapowers for the fix waves was sound: cycle-2 ran 10 tasks in 2 waves with one correctly-resolved fix round and clean merges. The mismatch was at the layer above: a /goal Stop-hook loop kept re-dispatching three parallel adversarial reviewers per cycle, and 'three adversarial reviewers probing an input grammar will essentially always find one more exotic variant.' Parallel breadth that helps a single fix wave becomes a cost multiplier when the loop never converges. The agent recommends 'capping iterations or weighting findings by realistic impact next time.'
implication: The routing lens should distinguish 'is parallel execution right for this wave' (yes here) from 'is the loop driving repeated parallel execution well-founded' (no here). A convergence/termination check belongs in the loop/goal harness, not in the per-wave engine. Document that parallel fan-out under a fixed-point stop condition needs an explicit iteration cap.
title: ultrapowers was the right call for cycles 1-4 (parallel reviewers found bugs), but the meta-loop wrapped around it was the wrong control structure — parallelism amplified a non-terminating condition

evidence: Mid/post-run the slice shows: 'fatal: \'main\' is already used by worktree at \'/Users/marcusestes/Websites/ultrapowers/.claude/worktrees/wf_6d81ea7c-6ff-12\''. The worktree list confirms it: wf_6d81ea7c-6ff-12 sits at 9fe1e37 [main] while five sibling engine worktrees are at detached HEADs. All four waves still MERGED successfully (194 tests green), so the run succeeded, but an engine worktree bound to the local main branch blocks the session from checking out main and litters the worktree list until swept. This is the documented worktrees-bind-to-session-repo / post-run-checkout-drift hazard recurring live.
implication: The setup/merge agents (or sweep) should never leave an engine worktree holding the local default branch. Engine guidance should ensure worktrees are cut from a detached integration ref, never 'main', and the post-run sweep must reclaim any worktree pinned to the default branch. ultralearn should flag this recurring 'main-bound worktree' signature as known friction.
title: An engine worktree got checked out to 'main', producing 'fatal: main is already used by worktree' and leaving the session main checkout blocked after the run

evidence: Compiler output: 'waves: [[\'1\',\'5\'],[\'2\'],[\'3\'],[\'4\']]' with edges 1→2,2→3,3→4 (markers) PLUS compiler-confirmed write-collision edges 1→3,1→4,2→4 because all four HTML tasks edit the same evals.html file. The assistant narrated this plainly: 'all four HTML tasks edit the same file, so they serialize — the engine's own degrade rule, applied to itself.' Real parallel breadth was a single wave of two tasks; the remaining three waves are one task each. The run took 21 min / 18 agents / 619k tokens for a plan whose shape gives parallelism almost nothing to do.
implication: Positive: the write-after-write serialization correctly prevented same-file corruption (the degrade rule worked). But the routing/planning signal is that a plan where N tasks all edit one file is a poor fit for the parallel engine — the wave shape collapses to a chain plus engine overhead. ultraplan guidance could warn at authoring time when most tasks share a single file, so the operator knows parallelism won't pay off before approving the wave plan.
title: All four HTML tasks edit the same file, so the 'parallel' run self-serialized into a near-chain — only Task 1 ∥ Task 5 ran truly concurrently

evidence: Task 7 (export GIFs/MP4s via Playwright/ffmpeg, eyeball for smooth loops) was classified out of the task set and 'presented after the merge gate' as the post-merge runbook, exactly as the disposition contract intends. At the gate the assistant explicitly listed 'Still unverified by anyone: the Playwright/ffmpeg export path itself — that's Task 7, manual by design,' then after approval the operator/agent ran render_post_media.py, hit a transient 'No such file' (script not yet on the checked-out branch) and a path reset, then produced the media and verified frames. Clean self-limiting behavior: the engine did not attempt the eyeball/binary-tool step headless.
implication: Exemplar of the runbook disposition working as designed — release/manual work stays human-gated. Keep as a calibration positive. Minor: the post-merge runbook step initially failed because render_post_media.py wasn't on the checkout yet, a small ordering nit worth noting but not a defect.
title: The release/manual task (Playwright+ffmpeg media export, needs eyeballs) was correctly held out of headless execution and handed back to the operator as a post-merge runbook

evidence: The visualization plan executed cleanly (5 tasks, 4 waves all MERGED, 0 fix rounds, all reviews clean), but the harvested slice is mostly large pasted files: the ~585-line swarm-viewer HTML/JS, a ~600-line 'field notes' eval blog post (docs/superpowers-ultrapowers-evals.html), the entire ~360-line Imagine/visual-creation-suite skill reference, and the finishing-a-development-branch skill doc. The wave-execution turns (Workflow launched wf_6d81ea7c-6ff, the merged-waves JSON, the pre-merge report) are a small fraction. Like run 1, this is a home self-development session about ultrapowers' own docs/viewer.
implication: harvest_runs.py's slice extraction pulls full pasted-file user turns verbatim, so sessions that paste large artifacts (viewer HTML, blog posts, skill docs) produce slices with poor execution-signal-to-noise. The harvester should truncate or summarize large pasted-file user turns (keep a head + size marker) so the wave/gate/routing lines aren't drowned, and readers should be warned that home meta-sessions carry this bloat.
title: Meta-session with extreme artifact bloat: the slice is dominated by pasted viewer HTML, a full eval blog post, and an entire visual-creation skill doc — burying the actual wave execution

evidence: Every agent in this bundle's audit.agents carries role 'impl:?' or 'review:?' with the task id stripped (e.g. impl:? haiku 58 turns, review:? opus 33 turns), unlike run 5f0be80b3c2fc847 where roles are precise (impl:4, review:5, etc.). misrankCandidates is empty. The pre-merge report in the slice DOES have the per-task tier table (Task 1 Sonnet adversarial, Task 5 Haiku lean, etc.), so the information existed at runtime but the audit harvester could not bind agents to task ids for this transcript layout.
implication: audit_run.py / harvest_runs.py fail to recover the task-id from this run's agent labels, defeating per-task tier-misrank detection. The harvester should fall back to parsing the orchestrator's own pre-merge tier table (which is structured and present) when agent-label task-ids are unrecoverable, so the cost lens isn't blinded.
title: impl agents lost their task-id labels in the audit (all 'impl:?' / 'review:?'), so per-task tier and misrank analysis is impossible for this run

evidence: An eval-result JSON embedded in this session's slice (run_id degrade-B-1) notes: '16min operator idle occurred post-gate (phone call) and is excluded; operator accidentally approved gate after idle - integration branch intact, not merged'. The gate caught it (nothing merged to main, integration branch intact), but it shows the pre-merge human gate can receive a mis-click / stale approval when the operator returns from a long idle. The recurring probe-then-run double-launch (preflight wor7e4leo/wf_80175f51, then run wgwgmgrw4/wf_6d81ea7c) also briefly confused the live narrative as 'two workflows launched'.
implication: The pre-merge gate is the one irreversible human step; an accidental approval after idle is a real failure mode. The engine could require a fresh re-render / re-confirmation of the report if a long idle elapsed before approval, or make 'Approve' a two-step confirm for the merge-to-main path. ultralearn should track 'accidental/idle gate approval' as an operator-friction signature.
title: The gate is fragile to operator idle: a separate run in this project recorded the operator accidentally approving the merge gate after a 16-minute idle (phone call)

evidence: Pre-merge report: 18 agents, ~619k subagent tokens, 21 minutes, 7 commits, all five tasks 'clean' with 0 fix rounds, baseline 177 → 194 tests on the integration branch with zero skips, all four waves merged without conflicts, judgmentCalls [], unfinished [], blockedWaves []. Tiering held: Sonnet on the two adversarial-reviewed tasks (engine + race figure), Haiku on the three lean tasks (bodies carried verbatim code), Opus reviewers throughout. The completeness critic correctly flagged that nothing had executed the animations in a real browser, which the orchestrator then closed at the gate via the capture API.
implication: Positive exemplar of a clean tiered run with the completeness critic catching a real gap (static tests never ran the animation) that the orchestrator closed before approval. Reinforces that the completeness-critic + tiered-review design pays off; keep as a calibration positive. Few of these needed.
title: Clean run, strong parallelism payoff on the one wide wave: 0 fix rounds, all reviews clean, baseline 177 → 194 tests, no judgment calls

evidence: ee606d8dd8880e24 and 85db3966b820b191 share planPath (docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-11-open-issues-batch.md), the identical transcriptDir (…/wf_df7eefdb-7b1), an identical 26-agent audit block and totals (794 turns / 86803 tokens), and byte-for-byte identical slice.md except the final assistant turn (ee606 has the full closeout, 85db is truncated mid-paragraph). So one underlying run produced two bundles with two different runIds. Worse, ee606's bundle.json sessionId is 657332b8-acca-… while its transcriptDir lives under session e21b741f-23fa-… (which is exactly 85db's sessionId) — ee606's own sessionId does not match its own transcriptDir.
implication: harvest_runs.py is double-emitting one run and mis-keying sessionId on one copy; downstream readers/the ledger will double-count this run's findings and trust a wrong sessionId. Dedup bundles by (transcriptDir / workflow runId) and assert sessionId matches the transcriptDir's owning session before writing a bundle.
title: Two bundles (ee606… and 85db…) are the SAME run harvested twice — duplicate runIds for one transcriptDir

evidence: This dev session launched /ultrapowers twice for real: wf_df7eefdb-7b1 (10 tasks, 3 waves, 26 agents, ~939k tokens, ~16 min) and later wf_9179965c-e37 (3 tasks, 2 waves, 10 agents, ~8 min — slice lines ~4607-4666). The bundle.json transcriptDir and audit.agents[] reflect ONLY wf_df7eefdb-7b1; the 10-agent second run and its first-live audit_run.py table are absent from the audit totals. The cost lens therefore under-reports the session by a whole parallel run.
implication: A single harvested 'run' is being equated with a single session, but one session can host multiple /ultrapowers launches. Harvest should emit one bundle per workflow runId (wf_*), not one per session, and the audit must scope to that workflow's own subagents dir.
title: Audit block captures only the FIRST of two real /ultrapowers runs in the session — second run is invisible

evidence: The 4683-line slice carries verbatim dumps of compile_plan.py (lines ~1229-1876), sim_workflow.mjs, sweep tests, the full wave-plan JSON with every task body inlined (~2619-2760), and the AskUserQuestion echo turns — all 'user' turns from the dev workflow. Only ~20 assistant turns carry the run's actual arc (gate, report, approve). A reader must hunt past ~2000 lines of code paste to reach the run report at line 2795.
implication: This is the meta-session hazard the brief warns about: a HOME dev session that authors the engine produces transcripts where 'user' turns are tool echoes / file reads, not operator intent. harvest's slice heuristic (user turns + wave/gate/routing lines) over-captures pasted source here. Add a filter that drops large fenced/source-file pastes and tool-result echoes from the slice, or cap per-turn bytes, so the operator/gate signal survives.
title: slice.md is dominated by planning-session source-file pastes, drowning the actual run narrative

evidence: Step-5 report (slice ~2795-2812): Task 7's reviewer independently verified that under --separate-git-dir, `git worktree list --porcelain` reports the git dir not the working tree — 'my plan's snippet alone would have failed its own test' — and the implementer added a `--show-toplevel` fallback the reviewer confirmed was a correctness fix, not scope creep. Task 6's implementer added a justified `**Files:** none` carve-out 'the spec missed — the literal spec would have broken existing gate-task tests.' Both deviations were surfaced, judged, and accepted at the gate rather than silently absorbed.
implication: Self-correction beyond task scope is a desirable emergent behavior worth naming as an exemplar in the reading-lenses frontier guidance and in README — independent review caught defects the human planner introduced. No fix needed; capture it as a positive calibration example.
title: Implementer/reviewer pairs caught and fixed flaws in the orchestrator's OWN plan, not just their task

evidence: Compiler emitted waves [1,4,7,9]→[2,5]→[3,6,8,10] with 12 edges (9 marker + 3 inferred write-after-write), zero conflicts, zero cycles (slice ~2578-2599). The run finished all 10 tasks done, all 3 waves MERGED, 164 pytest + sim green, 0 fix iterations anywhere, ~16 min. Four file-collision lanes (workflow.js / compile_plan.py / sweep / docs) were explicitly shaped by the planner so same-file tasks serialized via Depends-on.
implication: Strong evidence the ultraplan marker contract + file-collision-lane discipline yields clean parallelization at 10 tasks. Routing recommendation matched outcome exactly. Keep as the canonical 'good marking' case; nothing to change.
title: 10-task / 3-wave plan compiled to zero marker conflicts and ran 0 fix iterations — clean parallel exemplar

evidence: The human's attention went entirely into deciding 11 issue dispositions via batched AskUserQuestion answers (slice ~443, 583-587) and approving the wave plan (2599) and the Step-5 report (2818) — exactly where the design intends. At the gate the operator simply pasted the sweep + git-log output and approved; no mid-run intervention, no surprise. A later operator aside ('was the model choice appropriate?') spawned the audit_run.py work (#20/#21).
implication: Confirms the attention-shift thesis works on a real batch: humans decided WHAT and HOW-VERIFIED up front, then only gated. The operator's spontaneous cost question becoming permanent telemetry infrastructure is worth noting as a healthy feedback path. No change.
title: Operator stayed in planning/gates; the per-issue AskUserQuestion batch front-loaded all decisions before any code

evidence: Before any /ultrapowers run, the session ran the prior shipped feature as a deterministic shakedown (slice ~1253-1299): every one of 6 Haiku relay agents put only the inner pytest text ('….20 passed in 0.01s') into the schema's {raw:string} field, dropping run_acceptance.sh's JSON envelope. Running the engine's exact extraction regex (waves.js:931, /\{[\s\S]*\}/ then JSON.parse) against each captured raw proved 6/6 false negatives: a genuinely green exam → status:ERROR, passed:false → the gate refuses to Approve every time. Filed #36 + #37.
implication: The most important agentic path (the acceptance relay) silently inverted its verdict, and only a hand-built shakedown — not the committed suite — caught it. ultralearn should treat 'verification path that relays structured output through a cheap model' as a standing hazard pattern in reading-lenses; and the engine fix (administer the exam deterministically at the gate via exit code, delete the relay) is the right structural answer.
title: A read-only SHAKEDOWN (no feature plan) discovered the sealed-acceptance relay false-negatived 6/6 — design did not anticipate the relay corrupting JSON-in-JSON

evidence: Pre-launch check (slice ~3274-3295): cached plugin 0.0.6 ships workflow.js; repo main 0.0.8+ ships harnesses/waves.js, and the two engines DIFFER. /ultrapowers normally launches the cached engine — meaning the run would NOT exercise the very code the plan fixes, and `**Acceptance:** suite` may be a no-op on 0.0.6. The agent recommended 'launch on 0.0.6'; the operator INTERRUPTED ('[Request interrupted by user]', ~3316) and insisted on running the repo-main engine, forcing a manual install of harnesses/waves.js as the saved workflow.
implication: 'The installed plugin lags the repo' is a known CLAUDE.md gotcha but here it nearly caused a self-test that exercised the wrong engine — and the engine's default recommendation was the unsafe one. SKILL.md Step 4 should detect cache-vs-repo engine skew when self-hosting and default to (or at least prominently flag) installing the repo engine. Worth a routing-hook/SKILL note.
title: Version skew: /ultrapowers would launch the stale 0.0.6 CACHED engine while repo main is 0.0.8+ (engine moved to harnesses/waves.js)

evidence: After installing repo-main waves.js/probe.js, .claude/workflows held TWO scripts with meta.name 'ultrapowers' (waves.js + stale 0.0.6-named ultrapowers-run.js) and two with 'ultrapowers-probe' (slice ~3443-3460). The registry resolves by meta.name, so duplicates make the launch ambiguous; the agent had to confirm ultrapowers-run.js was the stale 0.0.6 cache and remove the stale-named duplicates before exactly one resolved per meta.name. This is the documented #29/launch-by-meta.name gotcha biting live.
implication: Step 4a's idempotent install does not GARBAGE-COLLECT differently-named prior copies that share a meta.name — a rename across versions (ultrapowers-run.js → waves.js) leaves a duplicate that breaks resolution. Step 4a should remove any .claude/workflows script whose meta.name collides with what it is about to install.
title: Saved-workflow registry resolves by meta.name, so stale-named duplicate copies made the launch ambiguous and had to be hand-deleted

evidence: On run completion the agent wrote 'Before I trust the report (this repo has a history of the completeness critic reviewing the wrong tree — #29), I'll restore the session checkout and independently verify against git ground truth' (slice ~3587), then re-ran the full suite itself (252 passed), grepped for PENDING_GATE/relay-string removal, and only then presented the gate (~3612-3617). At the gate the operator chose 'Approve + fix comment + shakedown'.
implication: Earned distrust from a prior incident (#29) is now baked into the run's own behavior — the engine verifies the verifier. This is healthy and worth surfacing as a routine in report-format.md (the gate should always cross-check the report's claims against git on this project). Positive exemplar of verification-first culture.
title: Operator distrust drove independent git ground-truth verification of the report (because of the #29 wrong-tree history)

evidence: Completeness critic flagged waves.js:904-906 still reads 'the agent only relays its stdout (BAKE:ACCEPTANCE-EXAM)' describing the now-deleted relay (slice ~3623). 'The plan's Task 1 didn't list this line, so the implementer correctly stayed in scope.' The drift was real but the implementer did not opportunistically expand scope; the gate caught it and the operator fixed it as one explicit commit during finishing.
implication: A nice counter-example to scope creep: agents adhered to the literal task surface and the gate's completeness critic caught the residual drift instead. Reinforces that the completeness-critic-at-gate pattern catches scope-edge drift the implementer is told not to touch. No change; calibration example for reading-lenses.
title: Implementer correctly stayed IN scope and left a stale comment unfixed because the plan didn't list that line — self-limiting, surfaced at the gate

evidence: Post-run cleanup finding (slice ~4163): .gitignore intends to ignore the saved-workflow install artifacts but lists the stale 0.0.6 filenames (ultrapowers-run.js / ultrapowers-probe.js), while the repo-main install produces waves.js / probe.js — so the new names show up as untracked in git status. Also: the installed waves.js copy was a stale pre-fix snapshot (the old engine that ran the workflow, before the relay-deletion merged).
implication: The rename of the engine harness across versions left two trailing artifacts (gitignore patterns and an install snapshot) keyed to the old name. Update .gitignore to the harnesses/*.js install names and have Step 4a refresh the install snapshot post-merge. Small hygiene fix tied to the same rename as the meta.name-dup finding.
title: .gitignore ignores install artifacts under STALE 0.0.6 filenames, so repo-main install names (waves.js/probe.js) leak into git status

evidence: audit.agents[]: impl:1 (opus, 58 turns, 14815 tokens, adversarial ×2 reviews) handled the engine de-relay; impl:2 (sonnet, 30 turns) handled docs/skills; totals 224 turns / 36771 tokens; misrankCandidates: []. Tier choice matched stakes (most-capable+adversarial on the frozen-engine edit, standard+lean on prose). No fix iterations.
implication: Clean cost profile: parallelism payoff is modest at 2 tasks but tier/review allocation tracked task risk correctly and the audit found no misranks. Confirms audit_run.py misrank flagging behaves on a small run. No change.
title: Small 2-task fix run with empty misrankCandidates — adversarial review concentrated on the one engine-mutating task

evidence: Second embedded run report (slice ~4365): 'Task 4's worktree cleanup is instruction-only — baked into the merge prompt — and this run left 31 worktrees behind, more than the 16 the task set out to fix, because the integration was assembled by per-task merges where the cleanup instruction was never the actor. Worse, the Task-13 gate passed `git status --porcelain` only because Task 12's new .gitignore HIDES exactly the artifacts Task 4 was meant to delete — green over an uncleaned tree.' The critic recommended a non-stubbed end-to-end cleanup test.
implication: A prompt-baked side-effect ('the agent should clean up') is not a guaranteed action — only a deterministic actor (sweep_worktrees.sh) is. And a gate that greps git status can be defeated by a .gitignore added in the same run. This is the lineage that produced sweep_worktrees.sh and the locked-worktree work; ultralearn should flag 'verification asserted via a check a sibling task can subvert' as a hazard pattern. Engine: cleanup belongs to a deterministic post-run sweep, not a merge-prompt instruction.
title: Completeness critic found the run left 31 worktrees behind (more than the 16 the task set out to fix) because cleanup was instruction-only, never an actor

evidence: slice ~3227-3235: the workflow's setup agent checked out ultra/integration-20260610-markers in the SESSION checkout and never restored it, so after the run `git log` on HEAD showed all feature commits and `git merge` into itself returned 'Already up to date' — looking exactly like 'the work is already on main.' The reflog proved main was still at the pre-feature commit and nothing pushed before approval. The agent named it 'a UX hazard in the engine, not a gate breach' and noted 'Anyone running git log at the pre-merge gate would draw the same wrong conclusion you did.'
implication: The most dangerous failures here are perceptual, not actual: the gate held, but the operator's trust was nearly broken by a stale checkout. Fix shipped later (restore session checkout to baseBranch at run end / state it in Step 5). ultralearn should weight 'operator drew a wrong conclusion from engine-left state' highly under the operator+friction lenses — trust damage without a real breach.
title: Post-run session checkout left on the integration branch created a 'gate held?' illusion — operator thought work reached main when it had not (#32)

evidence: slice ~4220-4222: 'I tried to author the entire plan in a single Write call — 14 tasks including a ~250-line compiler, test files, and six documents of edit instructions — and one generation block that long hit the time limit before emitting anything. The fix was to write it incrementally (header + Task 1, then four append chunks), which is also a lesson worth keeping: the same discipline ultraplan imposes on plans applies to writing them.'
implication: A real generation-length ceiling on plan authoring, recovered without human help by chunking — exactly the brief's frontier calibration (an agent self-limiting against an impossible-in-one-pass task). writing-plans/ultraplan guidance should explicitly recommend chunked incremental authoring for large plans so the failure is avoided rather than recovered from.
title: Authoring a 14-task / ~1100-line plan in one Write call hit the generation time limit — agent self-corrected to incremental chunked writes

evidence: First embedded run (slice ~3029): 'the first launch died in 11ms with `meta is not defined` at workflow.js:97 — the committed script mutates meta.phases at runtime, but the current Workflow engine extracts the meta literal at parse time and no longer exposes it to the executing body. I wrapped the mutation in a `typeof meta !== "undefined"` guard … committed it to main (0d86241) … and relaunched. The dogfood run is already earning its keep.' (This is the same crash a downstream user later reported as #7 on the stale 0.0.1 release.)
implication: Self-hosting surfaced a parse-time/runtime engine-contract drift that no test caught (engine globals can't run in CI). The recurring theme across this batch: engine bugs that only appear at real launch (meta-binding, session-checkout drift, cleanup-not-an-actor, relay JSON-in-JSON) need live-shakedown coverage, since the committed sim stubs the engine. Worth a standing note that the sim cannot catch engine-host contract changes.
title: Self-hosting dogfood caught a fatal engine-eval crash live: 'meta is not defined' at launch (issue #7), fixed and relaunched mid-session
