evidence: Second-run report (slice ~4363): 'the task-3 reviewer caught that its branch was parented on the merge-base and re-introduced task-2's entire changeset — predicted the merge conflict, verified it was mechanically reconcilable, and mutation-tested the new sim scenario to prove it exercises real logic. The wave-2 merge then resolved exactly as predicted.' The reviewer also un-skipped the superpowers 5.1.0 compat attestation test and confirmed the installed==attested version.
implication: Reviewer behavior exceeded the prompt: it reasoned about git topology (wrong-parent worktree → predicted conflict) and validated test quality via mutation testing, not just correctness. This is exactly the 'stale-base worktree' hazard the engine's BASE-anchoring guard addresses; reviewers actively defending against it is a strong exemplar. Capture under frontier as emergent reviewer rigor.
title: Adversarial reviewer predicted a wave-2 merge conflict by detecting a branch parented on the merge-base, and mutation-tested its own new scenario

evidence: Second embedded run: 12 implementation tasks across 3 waves, wave 1 merged all 8 branches without conflict, 55 pytest passed, 38 agents, ~1.87M tokens, 47 min, only 2 fix-loop iterations total (task-1 compiler, task-6 drift tests). Bundle audit.totals for the harvested (first) run: 607 turns / 86125 tokens across 22 agents, misrankCandidates: []. Implementers spanned opus/sonnet/haiku by task stakes.
implication: Demonstrates the engine scales to a 12-task, 8-wide wave at ~1.9M tokens while staying clean — a useful upper-end frontier datapoint for 'how large did work get and still succeed.' Note again the cost-lens gap: the bundle audit only sees the first embedded run (22 agents), so the 38-agent run's economics are invisible to ultralearn's telemetry.
title: 47-minute, 38-agent, ~1.87M-token wave run with only 2 fix iterations — large parallel batch sustained cleanly

evidence: First-run completeness finding (slice ~3045): 'in plan-markers.md, the marker-syntax example was written unfenced — a raw `### Task 4: Wire the health probe` heading sits in the document body. Deliciously, that's the exact fence-aware hazard the same file warns about, and my arg-encoding of the task body ("format as a fenced markdown block") was evidently too easy to misread.' One-line fix: wrap the example in a ```markdown fence.
implication: A documentation example demonstrating a hazard fell into that very hazard, and a task-body instruction ('format as a fenced block') was misread by the implementer. Reinforces ultraplan authoring rule 1 (wrap embedded examples in fences) — the rule must be applied to the docs that TEACH the rule, and task bodies that describe formatting should show the fence, not describe it.
title: Plan-marker example was authored UNFENCED — the exact fence-aware hazard the same file documents — caught by the completeness critic

evidence: The slice for e09bb5ed is a single human-driven session that opens with the superpowers /verify skill, then brainstorming, writing-plans, and ultraplan — it DISCUSSES and PLANS audit-drawer fixes; it contains no Run-launched/Run-complete/wave-merge/gate text. Yet bundle.json's audit{} block (16 agents: impl:1-4, review:1-4, merge x2, setup) is the audit of a SEPARATE workflow (wf_fa2b2a99-92f, the run that BUILT the audit drawer) — its transcriptDir points at that workflow, not at this verify session. So the lens evidence (slice) and the cost evidence (audit) come from two different runs glued into one bundle. This is exactly the meta-session hazard the brief warned about.
implication: harvest_runs.py is correlating a planning/verify session to a workflow audit and emitting them as one bundle. Tag bundles with a 'session kind' (execution vs planning/verify/meta) and either keep slice+audit provenance separate or drop pure-meta sessions, so readers do not attribute a dev session's text to an execution run's audit.
title: Harvested 'run' is a /verify dev session, not a wave execution; audit block describes a DIFFERENT run

evidence: The verify session rendered the audit drawer from the very run that built it and returned FAIL: '2 of 3 headline promises don't work.' Stations were non-clickable (0 of 4) because audit_run.classify looks for '### Task N:' in the agent's first user message, but the real engine prompt says 'find the object whose "id" is "N"' and keeps the task body in a separate JSON file, so 10 task agents were unreachable. The --embed SNAPSHOT build was dead from 6 SyntaxErrors: transcripts contain literal '</script>' and json.dumps doesn't escape '<' or '/', so the first baked '</script>' closed the inline script early. The existing tests passed only because their fixtures encoded a '### Task N:' shape the engine never emits.
implication: Tooling that classifies agents by regex-sniffing transcript prose is inherently fragile and drifted silently against the real prompt; the fix the session itself proposed (have the engine write role/task into agent-*.meta.json at spawn, where it already writes agentType/worktreePath) is the durable pattern. For ultralearn's own reader pipeline, do not trust transcript-text heuristics to identify roles — prefer engine-emitted metadata. Also: regression fixtures must encode REAL prompt/transcript shapes, not invented ones.
title: /verify FAILed the audit-drawer feature on its own canonical run — a sealed-style cross-check the engine couldn't do itself

evidence: The handoff offered /ultrapowers as option 1, normally authorizing immediate launch. The assistant explicitly refused to auto-launch: 'I explicitly held on the concurrency hazard, and I shouldn't launch a run that does heavy git surgery (integration branch + detaching the primary checkout + per-task worktrees) while another session can switch branches under it. That's the one thing only you can resolve.' It then invoked superpowers:using-git-worktrees to move the whole session into an isolated worktree before launching. The run's transcriptDir confirms execution happened inside ...--claude-worktrees-audit-drawer.
implication: The CLAUDE.md 'Serialize them / concurrent runs corrupt each other's checkout' gotcha surfaced as a real operator interruption in the design's no-pause launch path. The launch should preflight for concurrent-session / dirty-checkout risk and, when detected, route the operator into worktree isolation automatically rather than relying on the assistant to remember to hold. Consider a documented pre-launch concurrency check in SKILL.md Step 4.
title: Operator picked /ultrapowers but the assistant HELD the launch on the concurrency hazard, forcing manual worktree isolation

evidence: On compiling the audit-drawer plan the assistant found: 'it surfaced one thing my self-review must fix: an inferred 1 -> 2 prose-reference edge, which serialized the waves to [[1],[2],[3],[4]] and erased the genuine Task 1 || Task 2 parallelism.' After fixing the marker the plan compiled to [[1,2],[3],[4]] with 0 heuristic flags. The same prose-reference-edge mechanism is documented across the ultraplan slices as a conflict surfaced at the wave-plan gate.
implication: A backticked mention of another task's file/symbol without a matching Depends-on makes the compiler infer a prose-reference edge that needlessly serializes parallel work; the author caught it only by manually re-reading the compile output. ultraplan's authoring rules and self-review should make 'check every inferred prose-reference edge against intended parallelism' an explicit checklist item, and the wave-plan transparency render should highlight heuristic/inferred edges more loudly.
title: Compiler inferred a spurious prose-reference edge that serialized two genuinely-parallel tasks (caught in self-review)

evidence: The Live+Fractal viewer run merged all 4 waves (tasks 1-4, then 5+8, then 6, then 7) with every wave MERGED, suite green 334 passed/3 skipped, completenessFindings all VERIFIED on tree 9903f1f, judgmentCalls [], unfinished [], blockedWaves []. 26 agents, 158K output tokens. The completeness critic detached to the merge HEAD, confirmed git rev-parse HEAD matched, and resolved every escalated CANNOT-VERIFY item by reading the integrated tree.
implication: Positive exemplar of how large the engine can go and still land clean: the adversarial/lean review + CANNOT-VERIFY escalation + completeness-critic-on-detached-HEAD chain held at 8 tasks/26 agents. Worth preserving as a reference point for how complex a single approved plan can be before reliability degrades.
title: 26-agent / 838-turn run executed an 8-task, 4-wave plan with 0 fixes, 0 blocks, 0 judgment calls — a large clean parallel run

evidence: The completeness critic wrote: 'GENUINELY UNTESTED PATH (not a defect — out of automated scope by design): the LIVE d3-zoom wheel/pinch branch is never exercised by any test because the DOM stub forces reduced-motion=true... the 600ms CSS camera fly-in, visual split-pane rendering, station->meso->commit->micro focus... are runtime/visual behaviors requiring a real browser. The code paths are present and statically consistent... but actual in-browser rendering... is confirmable only by manual eyeball, which a static+node-stub review cannot perform.' It recommended the human do one browser eyeball rather than claiming PASS on unseen behavior. The same self-limiting recurs in fd94 ('the one thing no headless review can confirm is the live visual').
implication: This is the calibration behavior the brief names: an agent declining to assert coverage it cannot earn. The engine has no first-class channel for 'verified-by-construction but needs human eyeball'; today it lands as freeform prose in completenessFindings. A structured report field (e.g. visualEyeballItems[]) surfaced at the gate would let the operator's attention go exactly where the design intends — to the residual visual gap — instead of scanning prose.
title: Reviewers self-limit on browser-only visual paths, explicitly flagging 'GENUINELY UNTESTED PATH (not a defect)' and deferring to manual eyeball

evidence: bundle.json audit.misrankCandidates lists impl:5 (sonnet, 52 turns, 4326 out-tokens) and impl:1 (sonnet, 50 turns, 10496 out-tokens) — both above 1.5x the same-model median. The two heaviest agents overall were opus impl:6/impl:7 at 67 turns each (20023 and 13463 tokens). The run was otherwise clean (0 fixes), so the long sonnet turns reflect tasks that strained the cheap tier rather than failure.
implication: audit_run's tier-misrank flag fired on real data: two sonnet tasks did opus-sized work. The design intends these flags to 'feed the NEXT plan's tier assignments,' but that loop is manual and easy to skip. ultralearn could persist misrankCandidates into the ledger so tier hints accumulate across runs (relevant to the parked #21 plan-marker Tier: hints), turning a per-run advisory into a cross-run signal.
title: Two sonnet implementers flagged as tier-misrank candidates (52 and 50 turns) in a clean run — cheap tier ran long

evidence: Step-5 guard ran 'git checkout main' and got: "fatal: 'main' is already used by worktree at '.../.claude/worktrees/wf_9b203eca-57c-9'". The session repo was left at detached HEAD 9903f1f (the setup agent's integration checkout, 'which nothing switches back, exactly as the skill warns'). Guard 2 still passed (integration HEAD == waveMerges[last].headSha, no #29 drift), and the post-Approve sweep_worktrees.sh cleared the stuck worktree.
implication: Engine cleanup left a worktree pinning the base branch, so the documented checkout-restore guard cannot run as written until the sweep. The gate sequence assumes 'restore baseBranch' succeeds; it should tolerate (or pre-sweep) a base branch still held by a leftover engine worktree, or the guard ordering should put the sweep before the restore in this case.
title: At the gate, a leftover engine worktree still held 'main', blocking the checkout-restore guard

evidence: The pre-merge report's honest note: 'T3 — a weak assertion I wrote: my plan's assert "concentric" not in html is a tautology (that token never existed at base), so that one line doesn't really prove radial removal.' The work was still correct (other assertions + the completeness critic independently confirmed the radial primitives were gone), but the plan-authored test gave false assurance. The reviewer caught it; the test gate did not.
implication: A plan author can write an assertion that passes vacuously (token absent at BASE), and only the per-task reviewer noticed. writing-plans/ultraplan self-review should include a 'does this assertion fail RED at BASE?' check for absence-assertions, mirroring the sealed-exam RED-proof discipline that ultraplan already requires for the held-out exam.
title: Human-authored plan shipped a tautological test assertion ('assert concentric not in html') that proved nothing

evidence: audit.misrankCandidates is empty and the report says 'No tier-misrank flags,' yet impl:3 (opus, 112 turns, 32856 out-tokens — the template rewrite) was nearly 2x the next agent (sonnet impl:2 at 59 turns). The misrank heuristic only flags an implementer above 1.5x the median of SAME-MODEL peers, and T3 was the lone opus implementer, so it had no peer and could not be flagged.
implication: The tier-misrank flag is blind to single-sample tiers: a heavy opus task in a run with no other opus implementer is structurally unflaggable. The advisory is honest (it correctly refuses to flag a lone sample) but the cost signal — one task carrying ~half the run's tokens — is real and lost. ultralearn's cost lens should capture 'dominant single agent' independently of the same-model-peer requirement.
title: Single opus task (T3, 112 turns / 32856 tokens) dominated a 13-agent run but went unflagged — no same-model peer to compare

evidence: The planning session quotes an eval issue verbatim: 'report.py aggregates suite green from the integration branch's own test run. Run mixed-B-2 failed 4 of 7 held-out acceptance tests (tasks 4-6 never merged), yet its integration suite was 28/28 green — only the tests that exist can fail, so a half-merged plan reads as green. The mixed-B row... shows suite green 3/3, which misleads exactly the way the run's own completeness critic warned about ("green but incomplete").' The session's whole purpose was to add a coverage column (tasks_merged / tasks_planned) and a ⚠ flag when suite-green but coverage < 100%.
implication: The engine's strongest failure mode: when later tasks fail/cascade-block and never merge, their tests are absent, so the integration suite goes green on a partial implementation — the gate's headline signal lies by omission. Coverage (merged/planned) must ride next to suite-green in the pre-merge report itself (not only in the offline eval report.py), so the operator at the human gate sees incompleteness, not just green. This is the deepest 'verification can't check what isn't there' gap in the design.
title: A prior ultrapowers run's completeness critic warned 'green but incomplete' — half-merged plan passed its own suite 28/28

evidence: From the issue text in the planning session: 'mixed-B-2: the sonnet implementer duplicated then deleted schema.py, failed the fix loop, cascade-blocked tasks 5-6 -> 3/7 acceptance.' and 'mixed-C-1: same trap, opus recovered with 1 fix round (the only fix round in the C run).' The root cause was a latent undeclared dependency — the mixed fixture's Task 4 declared Depends-on: none but its spec required returning a schema.User from Task 1 — which the session decided to promote into a dedicated 'flawed' fixture (issue #27).
implication: A single tier choice flipped the outcome (sonnet failed the fix loop and cascade-blocked, opus recovered in one fix round) on the same trap — direct evidence that tier assignment on dependency-tangled tasks materially changes reliability, and that an undeclared Depends-on edge is the trigger. Reinforces both the prose-reference-edge / Depends-on discipline (the trap was a missing declared dependency) and the value of tier hints on risky tasks (parked #21).
title: Documented cascade-block: a sonnet implementer duplicated then deleted schema.py, failed the fix loop, blocked tasks 5-6 (3/7 acceptance)

evidence: At the brainstorming gate the operator answered three issues at once: #27 'Promote to flawed fixture'; #28 (engine-versioning matrix span) 'I'll take your recommendation'; #26 (flag suite-green-but-coverage<100%) 'I'll take your recommendation on this too.' The design intends humans to stay closely involved in planning; here the operator engaged on the concrete fixture decision but delegated the two methodology calls to the assistant.
implication: Consistent with the design's thesis (humans own WHAT and HOW-verified, agents own implementation), but it shows the planning gate degrading toward rubber-stamp on the more abstract methodology questions. ultralearn's operator lens should watch for 'I'll take your recommendation' as a signal that a decision the design wanted human-owned was effectively delegated, so the proposal layer can flag where attention actually went vs. where the design intended it.
title: Operator deferred three substantive design decisions to the assistant ('I'll take your recommendation') at the planning gate

evidence: bundle.json audit for bcb18 reports 16 agents / 570 turns / 78747 out-tokens for wf_fa2b2a99-92f, but the slice is the planning+concurrency-hold+release session (the actual wave execution happened inside the audit-drawer worktree and is not in the slice). Across this batch every bundle pairs a planning/dev slice with a workflow audit block: in e09 the mismatch is total (planningFound:false on a session that obviously planned). A reader cannot tie agent-level cost in the audit to any turn visible in the slice.
implication: Recurs across multiple runs in this project: the harvested slice (planning/integration session) and the audit block (headless workflow) are from different transcripts, so the cost lens is data-rich but un-cross-checkable against the friction lens. harvest_runs.py should either also slice the workflow's own subagent transcripts (the execution turns) or clearly label that slice and audit have separate provenance, so cost findings aren't silently attributed to the wrong turns.
title: Bundle audit and slice describe different runs, so the cost block cannot be reconciled against the visible work (recurs across runs in this project)

evidence: A 13-task / 5-wave / 38-agent / ~2.26M-token / ~77-minute run merged every wave cleanly, then the final completeness/integration critic hit a transient `API Error: Overloaded` and `agent()` RETURNED null (it does not throw on terminal failure). The engine's try/catch only handled thrown errors; the null result flowed past the catch and `review.testsPassed` was dereferenced at waves.js:1036, crashing the whole run AFTER all 13 task commits were already on the integration branch. The operator had to manually inspect the integration ref, re-run the suite (349 passed/3 skip), and only then could approve — then fixed the engine with a null-guard + regression test in the same branch.
implication: The engine treats `agent()` returning null as fatal even though the try/catch was written assuming agent failures throw. Add a null-guard at the completeness-critic dispatch site (and anywhere an agent result is dereferenced) that synthesizes the same `{testsPassed:false, findings:['critic did not run — verify manually']}` fallback used for the throw path, so a dead critic degrades to a gate judgment-call instead of crashing a merged run. This is the single highest-severity engine-robustness gap across the batch.
title: Final completeness critic returned null on API 'Overloaded' and crashed a fully-merged 13-task run

evidence: When the completeness critic crashed the run post-merge (see the null-deref finding), the operator did not lose 77 minutes of work: because acceptance was `suite` (acceptance.passed ≡ tests.passed), re-running the committed suite on the integration tree WAS the gate. The operator independently verified all deliverables on the integration ref (the new Interfaces-aware compiler even compiled its own plan end-to-end), got 349 passed / 3 intentional skips, and approved. The design's 'every claim checked by something it can't touch' thesis held precisely when the engine's own bookkeeping failed.
implication: Positive exemplar worth preserving: the `suite` disposition makes the orchestrator's own suite re-run a fallback for a non-running critic. Consider documenting in report-format.md / SKILL.md Step 5 that an absent/blocked completeness critic on a `suite` plan is recoverable by the orchestrator's independent suite run, so operators know the degraded path is sanctioned rather than improvised.
title: Verification-first design degraded gracefully when the engine itself crashed — suite re-run substituted for the dead critic

evidence: An ambitious 'max ambition on both parts' v6-integration + efficiency plan (Global Constraints/Interfaces parsing, Consumes/Produces DAG edges, pre-baked review packets, cannot-verify escalation channel, warm-cache worktree bootstrap, terse contracts) compiled to 13 implementation tasks across 5 waves, with two adversarial review passes on the four high-stakes engine/compiler tasks. All 13 merged cleanly, no reconciliation conflicts, only the post-merge critic crashed. This is the upper end of complexity the engine has carried without a single fix-loop or merge conflict.
implication: Calibration datapoint for how large a plan ultrapowers can absorb cleanly. The result (zero conflicts at 13 tasks) suggests the worktree-pure authoring contract + sibling-files threading is doing its job at scale; the only scaling-related fragility was the single unguarded agent-result deref, not the parallelism itself.
title: Largest run in the batch (13 tasks / 5 waves / 38 agents) succeeded on all work; the only failure was engine bookkeeping

evidence: audit.misrankCandidates flags impl:8 (claude-sonnet-4-6, 76 turns, 16411 outputTokens) — the highest output-token implementer in the run, above the opus impl:9/impl:11 (14.6k/14.1k). The run spent 1614 turns / 236k output tokens total. A sonnet task doing more work than the most-capable tasks is exactly the 'realized difficulty tracks spec risk, not diff size' pattern the tier criteria warn about.
implication: Confirms the standing audit signal feeds the NEXT plan's tier assignment: impl:8's task was under-tiered relative to its realized cost. Worth surfacing in the audit drawer / tier-hint discussion (#21) — one concrete recurring misrank per large run is enough evidence that plan-marker tier hints would help.
title: Lone tier-misrank candidate: a sonnet implementer ran 76 turns / 16.4k tokens, heavier than several opus peers

evidence: At release time the operator found marketplace.json still at 0.0.12 while plugin.json had moved ahead — 'a prior bump only touched plugin.json'. The 0.0.14 release re-synced both. This is the exact 'plugin.json wins silently if they drift' hazard CLAUDE.md warns about, observed happening in practice.
implication: The two-file lockstep bump is still done by hand and still drifts. A tiny release helper or a CI/pytest assertion that plugin.json.version == marketplace.json plugins[0].version would have caught the silent 0.0.12 lag before it shipped. Recurs as a class across releases in this project.
title: marketplace.json silently lagged at 0.0.12 while plugin.json was bumped — the documented lockstep-drift gotcha recurred

evidence: The run opened with the command's literal form `Invoke: Workflow({ name: 'ultrapowers', args: '<plan-path>' })`, which launched wf_5ebc581e-014; the engine immediately failed because `args.waves` was not populated ('The workflow needs compiled waves, not a raw plan path'). The assistant recovered by loading SKILL.md, compiling the plan with compile_plan.py, and relaunching correctly as wf_05aacb76-95e. The operator filed a standalone bug report at docs/bugs/2026-06-15-ultrapowers-command-collision.md. The SAME malformed launch form appears verbatim at the very start of run 2cabaadd0d8d652c — recurs across 2 runs in this project.
implication: The /ultrapowers command template hands the Workflow tool a raw plan-path string as `args`, contradicting SKILL.md's own Step 4b contract (args must be a compiled `{waves, edges, ...}` object). Every run currently 'works' only because the assistant notices the throw and re-routes through the skill — a wasted launch and a confusing first error each time. The command should invoke the skill orchestration (or pass nothing), never call Workflow directly with a path. This is a concrete, repeatable routing-surface fix.
title: /ultrapowers slash command launches Workflow({args:'<plan-path>'}) — malformed against the engine's args.waves contract; first launch threw

evidence: After the launch re-route, the real run (wf_05aacb76-95e) executed Wave 1 (serve_viewer.py) → Wave 2 (viewer offers) topologically serialized by the 1→2 marker edge, both reviews clean, 0 fix rounds, 325 passed. The Step-5 gate's #29/#32 drift guards (clean tree + integration HEAD == reported last-merge SHA) both passed; approved and shipped as PR #53.
implication: Baseline 'nominal' run for calibration. The only friction was the launch-form bug above; once past it, the small-plan path (one task per wave, lean review, suite acceptance) is smooth. No engine change implied beyond the command fix.
title: Clean small run (2 tasks / 2 waves) — exemplar of the happy path with drift guards exercised

evidence: In a 4-task / 3-wave / 16-agent run (sealed-acceptance env-bootstrap, 277 green, 0 fix rounds), the Task-1 implementer deviated from the plan's literal `test_collection_red_is_labeled`: the plan's test used `make_repo(feature_built=False)` against a suite importing `mod`, but `mod` still imports (its add() raises only at call time), so the test runs and is correctly classified `redKind:'assertion'` — meaning the plan's `assert redKind=='collection'` would have shipped a permanently-red suite. The implementer switched to a genuinely-absent module to force a real collection red; the adversarial reviewer AND the completeness critic both confirmed the deviation is a correct fix to a bug in the operator's plan. The shipped suite is more correct than the plan that was authored.
implication: Frontier-grade self-correction: an implementer that declined to faithfully transcribe a wrong plan, plus a review layer that endorsed the deviation rather than flagging scope-creep. The 'do not paraphrase the task' implementer discipline did not prevent a justified, reviewer-blessed correction. Worth holding up as the canonical 'green != correct, and the engine catches it' example — and a caution that exact-transcription task bodies still need the implementer's judgment escape hatch.
title: Adversarial reviewer caught a real bug in the OPERATOR'S OWN plan — a test that would have shipped permanently red

evidence: A second ultrapowers run (audit-drawer, wf_fa2b2a99 / ultra/integration-20260615-160505) was operating in the SAME repo; it fast-forward-merged ba979f3 and moved this run's checkout mid-review. This run's reviewer detected and recovered, and the work was verifiably intact, but the operator had to SKIP the repo-wide sweep_worktrees.sh (it removes all engine worktrees repo-wide and would have damaged the other run) and instead manually remove only its own wf_624e1542-d2a-* worktrees and branches. Memory records this hazard sharpened in the sibling run: worktree-isolating the orchestrator is INSUFFICIENT; only serializing runs is safe.
implication: 'Concurrent runs in one repo are unsupported' is currently a prose warning the operator must remember; here it caused real mid-run checkout drift and forced a manual, targeted cleanup. A cheap lockfile (e.g. .claude/ultrapowers/RUN_LOCK with the live runId) checked at setup and at the Step-5 sweep would turn a silent corruption hazard into a loud refusal. sweep_worktrees.sh should also scope to the current run's wf_<runId>-* by default rather than repo-wide.
title: Concurrent /ultrapowers run in the same repo fast-forward-merged and moved this run's detached HEAD mid-review

evidence: The run shipped finding E: the sealed exam ran in a bare detached worktree with no env bootstrap, so a path-dependent suite failed at COLLECTION (ModuleNotFoundError) and run_acceptance.sh reported it as an honest red — a FALSE red silently neutralizing the gate. The fix records bootstrapCmd in manifest.json, runs it before the suite, and classifies failures three ways: EXAM_BOOTSTRAP_ERROR (env couldn't be prepared — never a feature red), redKind:'assertion' (a test ran and failed), redKind:'collection' (nothing ran). The seal-author proves RED through a shared --baseline core. The gate now distinguishes 'feature wrong' from 'environment broken'.
implication: A subtle correctness hole in the verification machinery itself (a false red is as damaging as a false green for a gate) was found by real use and closed at its source. Reinforces that the held-out-exam path needs the SAME env the implementers had — env parity, not just code parity, is part of trustworthy acceptance.
title: Sealed-exam false-red root-caused and fixed: bootstrap-before-suite + three-way red classification

evidence: The operator pointed /ultrapowers at `...-verification-role-discipline-design.md` — a design spec with no `### Task N:` headings, `**Files:**` blocks, or `- [ ]` steps. The assistant ran compile_plan.py which returned 'no \'### Task N:\' headings found', correctly refused to launch, and instead drove the full pipeline (writing-plans + ultraplan → marked plan → compile → run). The slice opens with the same malformed `Workflow({args:'<path>'})` command form seen in run 1b064e1b — recurs across 2 runs in this project.
implication: Step 1's task-shape gate behaved exactly as designed (a spec is not a plan; refuse and remediate). But the recurring confusion — operators aiming /ultrapowers at a spec — suggests the Step-1 'no approved plan' message could detect the design-spec shape specifically and offer the brainstorm→plan remedy by name, rather than the generic 'no plan found' text. The meta-session hazard is present: this session DISCUSSES ultrapowers and EXECUTES a real plan in the same transcript.
title: /ultrapowers invoked on a DESIGN SPEC (not a plan); engine correctly stopped at Step 1 then authored the plan in-session

evidence: This run shipped the #29/#32 verification-role-discipline fix (critic pinned to verified merge HEAD via detach-to-sha + rev-parse self-check; read-only review roles; gate-side clean-checkout + HEAD-match assertion). The completeness critic reported all clean, but — because #29/#32 are LITERALLY about a critic that falsely claims a clean tree — the operator independently verified with git (status --porcelain empty; integration HEAD 92118112 == reported merge headSha; git grep on the integration ref proved every deliverable present, old `const COMPLETENESS_PROMPT` GONE) and re-ran the full suite (252) before approving. The verification machinery verified its own delivery.
implication: Self-referential dogfood exemplar: the operator applied the very distrust discipline being shipped, treating the critic's 'clean' as a claim to be checked rather than believed. The clean-checkout + HEAD-match gate assertions are now load-bearing on every run. Confirms the design's thesis empirically.
title: The run's own pre-merge gate was the first to exercise the trust-the-tree assertion it installs — and the operator refused to trust the critic

evidence: 3 tasks / 1 wave / 11 agents / 307 turns / ~43k output tokens, all reviews clean, 0 fix rounds. audit.misrankCandidates is empty; the most-capable impl:1 (opus, 55 turns) was correctly the heaviest implementer, and the two sonnet implementers (impl:2/impl:3) ran 18/19 turns. Tier assignment matched realized cost.
implication: Positive cost exemplar: when the plan is small and well-classified, the tier mapping lands cleanly with no misrank. Useful contrast with the large run's lone sonnet misrank — small, well-bounded plans don't surface the tier-hint need that big plans do.
title: Clean 3-task run with correct tier ranking — opus impl:1 (55 turns) correctly the heaviest, no misrank candidates

evidence: An early dogfood of the 'ultra-driven-development' predecessor engine (runtime-authored workflow, fix-loop cap 3) was set up to run against a disposable canary repo (~/Websites/udd-live-test) while the session was rooted in the read-only spec repo (~/Websites/ultrapowers). Before running anything destructive, the operator launched a READ-ONLY binding probe (wf_254f228a-889) which proved definitively: a worktree-isolated agent's gitCommonDir was ~/Websites/ultrapowers/.git — i.e. isolation:'worktree' creates worktrees inside the SESSION repo, owned by the session .git, independent of whatever REPO literal was baked into the script. A guard-obeying agent would BLOCK (stalling); a careless one would build the feature inside the protected repo. The operator stopped, surfaced it as the architecture's hard invariant, and fell back to explicit `git -C <testrepo> worktree add`.
implication: This is the architectural root of the standing 'never carry the git target via args; run /ultrapowers from INSIDE the target repo' rule — discovered empirically by an agent that built a cheap read-only probe to validate a dangerous assumption before acting, rather than running and hoping. Calibration-grade self-limiting behavior (the brief's exact pattern). The durable engine consequence: nothing enforces session-root == intended-target, so the only safety is the invocation-context rule; a preflight assert could compare cwd-toplevel to the plan's repo and refuse on mismatch.
title: Read-only binding probe discovered isolation:'worktree' binds to the SESSION repo regardless of any baked target path — before any destructive op

evidence: bundle.json audit.agents lists only 2 'unknown'/opus agents (8+8 turns) under transcriptDir wf_254f228a-889 — but that is the READ-ONLY binding probe; the actual orchestration the operator ran (the git-worktree fallback, wf_c5469e68-451, with setup/implementers/reviewers/merge/critic) lives under a DIFFERENT workflow run id and was not harvested into this bundle. The cost lens is effectively blind for this run's real work.
implication: Harvest-completeness gap: harvest_runs.py keyed this bundle to the probe's transcript dir, so the substantive run's agents/turns/tokens are missing. When a session launches multiple workflow run ids (probe + real run, or run + redirect), the harvester should associate ALL of a session's wf_* transcript dirs with the bundle, or at least flag in audit.note that other wf_* dirs exist unharvested.
title: Audit captured only 2 synthetic-tier agents — the real fallback run's agents are under a different transcript dir than the harvested one

evidence: This debugging session traced 'Workflow "ultrapowers-probe" not found. Available: deep-research, code-review' to a registry snapshot-timing bug: the engine snapshots its saved-workflow registry ONCE at session start, but the old SKILL.md Step 4a copied the harnesses into .claude/workflows/ mid-session (and .claude/workflows/ is gitignored + plugins can't ship saved workflows, so it's empty at the snapshot). The first /ultrapowers in a fresh checkout therefore could not resolve its own probe/waves workflows. Fixed by moving harness install into the SessionStart hook (before the snapshot) and teaching Step 4a½ to distinguish stale-registry (restart session) from real engine drift (Step 6). Shipped as PR #45 / released 0.0.11.
implication: A whole class of 'engine won't launch' failures came from install-timing vs registry-snapshot ordering, not engine logic. The fix (SessionStart-hook install + a not-found-vs-drift branch at preflight) is the right shape; the durable lesson for ultralearn is that 'workflow not found' should never be diagnosed as engine drift — it is almost always a stale registry curable by a new session.
title: Fresh-checkout first /ultrapowers fails: harnesses installed mid-session aren't in the engine's once-at-startup saved-workflow registry snapshot

evidence: bundle.json has audit.agents == [] with note 'no agent-*.jsonl under ...wf_6caf403b-e43', planningFound false, and planPath 'plan-routing' (not a real plan path). The session is a debugging + release meta-session (trace the probe-not-found bug → fix → cut 0.0.11) that never launched a wave run; no agents ran. The cost lens is empty by construction.
implication: Harvest treated a non-execution debugging session as a run bundle with a placeholder planPath. The harvester should either skip sessions with no harvestable wf_* agent transcripts, or tag them explicitly as 'meta-session / no execution' so readers don't mine a cost/routing signal that isn't there. The signal here is real (the registry bug) but lives entirely in the slice, not the audit.
title: Empty audit block: a debug/release meta-session with no /ultrapowers execution, misattributed planPath 'plan-routing'

evidence: The session closes (as do 2cabaadd, 6aab94e6) by noting the fix 'goes live for /ultrapowers after the plugin cache re-resolves 0.0.11 (via /plugin) and a fresh session starts' — a step the agent cannot perform from inside a session. Across the batch, every released fix carries this same caveat: repo main is ahead of the installed plugin cache until a human runs /plugin interactively.
implication: The 'installed plugin lags the repo' gotcha is a standing operator friction at the END of every cycle — the work ships but the engine the operator is running stays stale. This is inherent to the plugin system, but ultralearn should note the pattern: self-hosted dogfood runs validate repo-main code, not the cache the operator's NEXT /ultrapowers will actually use, so a re-resolve reminder is load-bearing and easy to forget.
title: Every release ends with the same dangling action: 0.0.x won't be live in /ultrapowers until the operator re-resolves the plugin cache

evidence: The pre-merge report flagged: the critic claimed Tasks 2, 5, 6 'left no trace' — wrong. Mid-run an agent detached the primary repo checkout (~/Websites/ultrapowers) to a sha containing only tasks 1/3/4; the critic could not check out the integration branch (locked by the session's worktree) and reviewed that stale detached tree, reporting the missing half. Git ground truth: all six task commits reachable from integration HEAD, test_salvage_gate.py present, prose-reference in all four doc surfaces. The 182-vs-185 test counts pinpoint the divergence. Operator: 'the critic doesn't verify that the tree it reviews is the tree it was asked to review.' Filed as issue #29. The actual run was clean (6/6 done, 0 fix rounds) so a human who trusted the critic would have wrongly Redirected/Salvaged a fully-complete run.
implication: The completeness-critic prompt (COMPLETENESS_PROMPT in wave-merge.md / workflow.js) must make the critic ASSERT it is on the integration branch at the integration HEAD before reviewing — e.g. require it to echo `git rev-parse HEAD` and the branch name, and abort/flag if it cannot check out the integration branch rather than silently reviewing whatever tree it landed in. The merge agent already runs in the main checkout; the critic inherits that checkout's drift. Pin the expected HEAD sha into the critic's inputs so a mismatch is a hard error, not a confident hallucinated gap.
title: Completeness critic reviewed a STALE DETACHED tree and raised three false-alarm gaps

evidence: git worktree list during the run showed `/Users/marcusestes/Websites/ultrapowers  5a57a64 (detached HEAD)` — the PRIMARY checkout, not an engine worktree, sitting on a mid-run sha. A later `git checkout claude/admiring-hopper-a6aa54` failed: 'fatal: branch already used by worktree at .../wf_b584519b-26a-6'. The assistant had to 'restore both checkouts (main in the primary repo, claude/admiring-hopper-a6aa54 here)' before presenting the gate. This is the same gotcha family the eval runs hit (integration branch left checked out, checkouts moved) and is the root cause of the stale-critic finding above — it recurs.
implication: Engine agents (merge, critic) running in the main checkout must never move the PRIMARY checkout's HEAD. Either pin the merge/critic to operate only inside a dedicated checkout, or have the workflow snapshot the primary checkout's branch/HEAD at launch and restore-and-verify it before the gate (the SKILL.md Step-5 restore is happening reactively, after corruption, rather than being prevented). Worth a guard in the merge/critic prompts: 'do not detach or move the repo you start in.'
title: Primary checkout detached mid-run by an engine agent — recurring worktree-coordinate corruption

evidence: '/.../python3: can't open file "/Users/marcusestes/.claude/plugins/cache/ultrapowers/ultrapowers/0.0.6/skills/ultrapowers/scripts/audit_run.py": [Errno 2] No such file or directory'. The effort audit was produced anyway ('20 agents, ~823k subagent tokens, 19.9 minutes'), so the orchestrator fell back, but the canonical script the run expected was absent from the resolved plugin version. Classic 'installed plugin lags the repo' (CLAUDE.md gotcha): the run resolved 0.0.6 while the repo/worktree carried newer scripts.
implication: Step-5 audit should not hard-depend on a script path inside the version-pinned plugin cache; either bundle audit_run.py with the version that references it (release-gating that the script ships) or have SKILL.md compute the effort audit from the workflow report fields directly so a cache/repo version skew degrades gracefully instead of erroring. Also a signal that self-hosting against a stale installed plugin is fragile.
title: audit_run.py missing from installed plugin cache (0.0.6) — script not found at Step 5

evidence: Launch note: 'integration branch ultra/integration-20260612-hardening cut from claude/admiring-hopper-a6aa54 (this worktree's branch — main is checked out in the primary checkout, so it cannot be the anchor here; logged as a judgment call).' The whole hardening was developed in a worktree, so the natural base (main) was unavailable as an anchor and the engine had to derive baseBranch=claude/admiring-hopper-a6aa54. This is a self-hosting-specific routing wrinkle the design did not call out.
implication: Document the worktree-self-host case in SKILL.md / dependency-analysis.md: when the desired baseBranch is checked out elsewhere, anchoring to the current worktree branch is correct but should be surfaced explicitly at the Step-3 gate (it was, as a judgment call). Consider auto-detecting this and recommending the worktree branch as baseBranch rather than leaving it to per-run judgment.
title: Integration branch could not anchor to main (checked out in primary checkout) — anchored to worktree branch as a judgment call

evidence: audit.misrankCandidates = [impl:3, sonnet, 59 turns, 7125 out-tok]; effort audit: 'One tier-misrank candidate: impl:3 (FILES threading, 59 turns) — the heaviest task, worth most-capable in future plans of this shape.' All six tasks were assigned standard tier up front ('the eval's tier-misrank lesson applied') yet realized difficulty still tracked spec risk, not the a-priori estimate — exactly the pattern reviewer-prompts.md line 180 records from run wf_df7eefdb-7b1. So even applying the lesson, the static tier estimate under-called the single threading-heavy task.
implication: The known issue (#20) that diff size doesn't predict difficulty keeps biting. Consider a turn-count / output-token signal fed back into the report's misrankCandidates so the operator can adjust tiers for the NEXT run, and consider whether prompt-threading tasks (touching workflow.js + reviewer-prompts.md + bake-drift tests) should be a recognized 'judgment-likelihood' class that auto-bumps to most-capable.
title: Tier-misrank recurred: heaviest task (impl:3, 59 turns) ran sonnet though it was the run's biggest implementation

evidence: This run is /ultrapowers (condition B, 0.0.6) executing 2026-06-12-eval-driven-hardening.md — a plan authored FROM the eval's mixed-B-2 cascade failure (Task 4 Depends-on:none while needing schema.User). The plan's own tasks add a prose-reference compiler edge, FILES scope guard, SIBLING FILES context, exact-assertion discipline, and a Salvage gate — each task body cites mixed-B-2 as the motivating failure. Result: 6/6 done, all clean on first review, 0 fix rounds, 0 blocked, 3 waves all MERGED, 185 tests passed. The engine fanned out and integrated its own hardening with no human implementation. Notably the plan itself was authored worktree-pure with prose-reference deps correctly declared (waves [1,3]->[2,4]->[5,6]) — it did NOT reproduce the undeclared-dep bug it was fixing.
implication: Strong positive exemplar of the design thesis: ambitious engine-internal change executed in parallel with the human only at planning + gate. The fact that the SAME run that hardens against undeclared deps was itself authored with deps correctly declared is the kind of self-consistency worth surfacing as a flagship dogfood case in README/report-format. No change needed; document it.
title: Recursive self-improvement: the engine cleanly executed the 6-task plan that hardens the engine against the very failure mode that produced the plan

evidence: Across the run the human's interventions are concentrated: approving the wave plan (choosing ultrapowers), then at the gate filing issues #26/#27 (eval follow-ups), #29 (the stale-critic dogfood bug), approving the merge, and PR #30. The assistant, not the human, detected the critic's false 'tasks 2/5/6 missing' claim by checking git ground truth and presented it as a known finding — the operator's job at the gate became 'read the report, file the bug, approve' rather than 'audit the diffs.' Wall clock 19.9 min, headless after launch.
implication: The pre-merge report correctly elevated a critic false-alarm to a prominent warning ('One finding you should know about') instead of burying it — good report design. Reinforces that the report must distinguish 'critic-asserted gap' from 'git-verified gap'; the assistant did this manually here, which argues for making git-ground-truth verification of critic findings a standard, automated section of the report rather than ad hoc.
title: Operator spent attention exactly where designed (gate + issue-filing), and caught the engine's false alarm rather than trusting the report

evidence: In a wide plan (~16 tasks, one wave of ~10 parallel), one implementer on the cheapest tier terminated without emitting its structured result — an infra/agent error, not a code failure, leaving no commit. Because two downstream tasks depended transitively on it, both were dep-blocked, so a clean first run still landed only ~13 of 16 tasks and one whole later wave was skipped. The shape: a non-deterministic transport/agent fault on one node propagates along the DAG and silently subtracts a feature subtree from the integration.
implication: The engine treats an empty/aborted structured-output return identically to a substantive task failure (no commit, dependents blocked). A cheap, bounded auto-retry of a task whose ONLY failure signature is 'no structured output emitted' (an infra hiccup, distinct from a real BLOCKED/failed verdict) would recover the common case without a full operator-gated salvage. Classify transport/agent faults separately from feature failures in the report.
title: Single agent-infra error (impl returns no structured output) cascade-blocks an entire dependency tail

evidence: After the partial first run, the integrated tree's full test suite passed (all tests + typecheck clean) AND the committed-suite acceptance disposition passed — yet the package was not end-to-end runnable: the missing modules had no surviving importers, so the existing tests/typecheck were green precisely because the consumers of the absent code were also absent. The independent completeness reviewer flagged this as a false completeness signal and the orchestrator refused to approve/merge despite both green gates. Shape: a verification surface the implementers can touch (the suite) said GREEN, and a surface they could not touch (the completeness critic reasoning about end-to-end reachability) overrode it.
implication: This is the verification-first thesis working as designed and is the single strongest positive exemplar in the batch: the held-out/independent critic is load-bearing precisely when a dependency-blocked subtree makes the committed suite pass vacuously. Worth codifying as a named pattern in the reading-lenses / report-format references: 'missing-module green' — when blocked tasks remove both producer and consumer, suite-pass is not completeness. Keep the completeness critic's reachability check prominent in the report so the operator sees WHY green is not done.
title: Completeness critic catches a FALSE-GREEN: test gate + suite acceptance both pass on a non-runnable package

evidence: The task that failed with the no-structured-output agent error had been tier-assigned to the cheapest model. On salvage the orchestrator bumped it exactly one tier up and it (and its two unblocked dependents) landed clean. The audit also flagged two mid-run tier-misrank candidates (a standard-tier task and a most-capable-tier task with comparable high turn/token counts), suggesting the initial tier estimates under-provisioned some nodes. Shape: cheapest-tier assignment on tasks that must emit a strict structured contract correlates with the protocol-adherence failure, and the recovery cost (re-run + reviews of the whole blocked subtree) exceeded the saving from the cheap first attempt.
implication: Tier assignment should bump tasks that must return a strict structured output (or are upstream of many dependents — high blast radius) off the cheapest tier by default, the same way the risk classes already bump small-diff tasks. Feed the audit's misrank candidates back into the next plan's tier defaults, and weight a task's fan-out (number of transitive dependents) into the tier decision, not just diff size.
title: Cheapest tier on a structured-output task is a false economy; salvage re-ran it one tier up and it landed clean

evidence: A task agent reported that two engine helper scripts it expected for worktree warm-up / review-packet generation were not present in its fresh worktree; it fell back to running the real dependency-install itself and hand-wrote the review packet into the shared git dir to replicate the expected output. The work succeeded, but the agent silently substituted its own setup for the engine's intended one. Shape: the harness assumes certain helper scripts are reachable from every isolated worktree, and when they are not, agents self-correct in undocumented ways rather than failing loudly.
implication: Either ship the warm-cache/review-packet helpers so they are guaranteed present in every isolated worktree (vendor them into the worktree, or invoke them from the common git dir), or have the setup role assert their presence and surface a loud diagnostic when absent. Silent agent improvisation of setup is exactly the kind of unreviewed-substitution the worktree discipline exists to prevent.
title: Worktree-isolated roles missing expected helper scripts; agents improvised a fallback setup

evidence: Two sibling tasks ran in the same parallel wave; the later one imported a symbol produced only by the earlier one, yet its declared dependency list was empty. The compiler's interface/prose-reference inference added the edge so wave ordering was still safe, but a reviewer separately recommended adding the explicit dependency edge because the task's own isolated typecheck could not pass without the sibling's output. Shape: author under-declared a real dependency; the engine's inference rescued execution ordering, but per-task isolated verification still tripped on the un-declared seam.
implication: The plan-authoring (ultraplan) self-review should harden the rule that any imported symbol another task produces requires an explicit Depends-on, and the engine should surface inference-rescued edges to the operator as 'authoring gaps to fix' rather than as silent informational inferences — the reviewer's complaint shows the cost lands on per-task verification even when wave ordering is fine.
title: A downstream task imported an upstream task's deliverable while declaring no dependency; compiler's inferred edge saved it but reviewer flagged the gap

evidence: Post-run, the agent's attempts to delete dead worktree/integration branches were blocked by a project-committed PreToolUse safety guard against destructive git commands. The agent diagnosed the guard correctly, declined to use an equivalent low-level command that would have evaded the substring match, explicitly named that evasion as 'quietly loosening a safety mechanism the team committed,' and instead offered the human three transparent options (run it yourself / scope the hook via a deliberate config change / explicit override). Shape: an autonomous agent hit a guardrail during cleanup, recognized that the clean workaround was a guard-bypass, and chose to escalate to the human rather than self-authorize.
implication: Strong positive exemplar of self-restraint the design did not explicitly script. Worth capturing in the ledger as desirable behavior; also a small product gap — the engine's own sweep step can be blocked by a host project's safety hooks, so the post-merge sweep should anticipate guarded environments and present the manual command for the operator rather than failing or pushing to bypass.
title: Self-limiting cleanup: agent refused to route around a committed destructive-git safety hook even though it blocked its own teardown

evidence: The human's qualitative arc tracked the intended division of labor: heavy involvement in spec/plan and in deciding measurement strategy upfront, then near-zero involvement during the wide parallel execution, then re-engagement at the pre-merge gate where the decision was framed around the completeness critic's false-green finding and a recommended salvage. The operator trusted the engine to fan out and only had to adjudicate the salvage-vs-pause choice. Shape: attention concentrated at the two human gates (plan, pre-merge) exactly where the design intends.
implication: Confirms the attention-relocation goal holds at this scale (~16 tasks). The gate presentation that worked led with the completeness verdict and a mechanically-constructed salvage recommendation; keep report-format leading with the completeness/false-green signal and an actionable salvage set rather than a raw per-task dump.
title: Operator stayed at planning/architecture altitude; attention at the gate went to the completeness verdict, not low-level diffs

evidence: For a core, hard-to-verify-by-reading integration path (~8 tasks, widest wave 4), the operator-facing routing rationale explicitly led with a risk override: independent per-task review and a single pre-merge gate mattered MORE than the (real but secondary) parallel speedup, and that override is what 'earned' the parallel engine for this plan. Shape: the routing recommendation was justified by verification value on an unreadable-by-inspection change, with parallelism as a bonus rather than the driver.
implication: Healthy signal that the routing heuristic weights 'hard to verify by reading' as a first-class reason to route to the parallel/independent-review engine, not just task width. Worth making this risk-override rationale an explicit, named branch in the routing hook's recommendation logic so it is reproducible rather than a per-run judgment call.
title: Routing chosen by a RISK override, not by parallel speedup — independent review + held-out check valued over fan-out

evidence: Before launch the orchestrator reasoned that a sealed held-out exam (authored from the spec alone, must go RED on base) was a poor fit here: the integration's interfaces were defined by the plan rather than the spec, so a spec-only author could not target them, and the one cleanly spec-derived behavior was already true on base and so could not go RED. It recommended an explicit waiver with a verbatim reason (real validation is a human/preview judgment; deterministic units covered by the plan's own committed tests) and offered a narrow alternative exam. The operator chose the waiver. Shape: the system declined to author a verification artifact that would have been vacuous, surfaced the reasoning, and got explicit operator consent rather than silently waiving.
implication: Mirrors the calibration example (a planner that declines to TDD against files that don't exist). The engine knowing WHEN a held-out exam cannot be meaningfully authored — and saying so with a verbatim, gate-surfaced waiver instead of producing a hollow exam — is the desired behavior. Capture 'plan-defined (not spec-defined) interfaces' and 'spec-derived behavior already true on base' as recognized waiver-justifying conditions in the sealing/disposition guidance.
title: Acceptance correctly WAIVED with reasoned argument that a held-out exam was ill-fitted to a plan-defined integration

evidence: The audit shows one task's implementer role appearing twice — once on a mid tier and once on the strongest tier with a separate turn/token count — consistent with a BLOCKED escalation to a more capable model rather than a retry of the same model. The run also spent a dedicated reconcile role (strongest tier) on a wave merge, indicating at least one merge/test conflict needed reconciliation rather than a clean barrier merge. Shape: the bounded fix-loop and wave-merge reconciliation machinery both fired on a core-path plan, adding real token cost beyond the nominal parallel speedup.
implication: Expected behavior firing as designed, but a reminder that escalation + reconciliation are where parallel runs spend their surplus tokens. The audit's role-doubling (same task, two tiers) is a clean signal of mid-task escalation — surfacing 'this task escalated a tier' explicitly in the effort table would let operators see where initial tiering was too low and feed it into next-plan defaults.
title: Implementer escalated to a stronger model mid-task, and a wave merge required a reconcile pass

evidence: The transcript slice for this run terminates immediately after the background workflow launch confirmation (run id, integration branch, 'I'll surface the report when it finishes') — the actual completion report, integrity checks, and pre-merge gate decision are not in the harvested slice, even though the run's per-agent audit (implementers, reviewers, merges, reconcile) IS populated from the workflow transcript dir. Shape: the conversation slice and the workflow-internal audit come from different sources, and the conversation slice can be cut off before the headless run reports back, so a reader sees the setup but not the outcome.
implication: The harvester should detect when a slice ends at a background-launch boundary (run launched, no corresponding completion/gate turn) and either pull the gate turn from a later session segment or flag the bundle as 'launch-only / outcome-missing' so readers don't mistake a truncated harvest for an abandoned run. Pair the conversation slice with the workflow report JSON when present.
title: Harvested slice for a launched run ends at launch with no captured completion/gate — observability gap for ultralearn itself

evidence: This bundle's metadata shows planningFound=false, a plan path placeholder rather than a real plan, and an audit of only two short 'unknown'-role agents (~13 turns total) — and the slice itself is a long research/brainstorming session: pasted documentation about the orchestration engine, a spec-authoring brainstorm, and exploration of how to depend on the upstream skill library. There is no wave plan, no parallel implementation, no merge, and no pre-merge gate anywhere in it. Shape: the harvester captured a design/research conversation that merely DISCUSSED the engine as if it were an execution run.
implication: harvest_runs.py is over-collecting: a 'run' with no waves/gate/launch and an audit of only unknown-role micro-agents should be classified as non-execution (research/planning) and either excluded or tagged distinctly, so readers don't spend a full five-lens pass on a session that never executed. Use planningFound=false + absence of a workflow launch + audit lacking impl/review/merge roles as the exclusion signal.
title: Harvested as a run but it is a research/spec-authoring session with no waves, no gate, and empty audit

evidence: The non-execution session in this batch is substantively the design conversation that produced the parallel-execution engine's own design spec — it brainstorms the conductor/engine seam, the worktree-isolation unlock for parallel implementation, the discipline-reuse-by-baking trick, and the human-gate-after-plan / gate-before-merge posture that the later real runs in this same batch then exercise at scale. Shape: ultralearn is reading, in one batch, both the origin design rationale AND two production runs that realize it, from a foreign project that adopted the engine.
implication: A genuine frontier observation for ultralearn's own value: pairing a design-rationale session with the runs that later embody it is high-signal for checking whether intended design properties (attention relocation, false-green catching, conservative DAG inference) actually held in practice. Worth letting the reader/distill layer link a project's design-session bundle to its execution bundles rather than scoring each in isolation — but only after harvest tags design sessions distinctly (see the over-collection finding).
title: This batch is dogfood-on-the-genesis-session: the harvested research run is where the engine itself was designed

evidence: The compiler output marked EVERY task heuristic:true and emitted no Type/Depends-on markers; the plan body carried the stock sequential-executor header, not the parallel-ready replacement. The operator ran the parallel engine on a plan authored with no parallel-execution contract, so every disposition and edge was inferred rather than declared. The run still waved and completed, but the whole interpretation rode on heuristics the author never confirmed.
implication: Detect at compile time that a plan carries zero markers and surface it loudly at the transparency render ('plan is fully heuristic — no ultraplan markers; all dispositions inferred'), nudging the operator to mark high-stakes plans. Consider whether the planning handoff should route unmarked plans differently.
title: Unmarked plan compiled entirely by heuristic, no ultraplan markers present

evidence: The last task declared an empty writes set (a build+QA verification step). The compiler drew an 'ambiguous-files' edge from each of the seven preceding implementation tasks into it, so it landed alone in the final wave gated on all prior work. A task that writes nothing is logically a gate, but because it was classified implementation it consumed a whole serial wave and a full implementer/reviewer cycle rather than compiling into run config.
implication: When an implementation task has an empty writes set AND only test/build/QA steps, the heuristics should lean toward gate classification (compiled into testCmd) instead of waving it as a fan-in implementation task. At minimum flag empty-writes implementation tasks at the render so the operator sees the serial tail before launch.
title: A final verification task with no write paths got an inferred edge from EVERY upstream task, forcing a fully serial tail

evidence: Five tasks ran in parallel in wave 1, but three separate tasks all wrote the same large route file, so write-after-write/write-after-create edges serialized them into three successive single-task waves after the fan-out. The genuinely independent work (new isolated hook/component files) parallelized cleanly; the route-file work could not, and the shape degraded to wide-then-narrow. The decomposition extracted a reducer into its own file precisely to reduce this contention, which helped but did not eliminate it.
implication: This is the classic shared-god-file contention pattern. ultraplan's seam-splitting guidance (cut along file seams; extract along responsibility) is the right lever — reinforce that the wave-plan render should highlight when N>1 tasks modify the same file so the operator can see the serialization the shape implies before launch.
title: Wide wave-1 fan-out followed by a forced single-file serialization chain on a shared route file

evidence: The audit block is fully populated (24 agents, ~1170 turns, ~141K output tokens) with a sensible tier spread: implementers across a cheap/standard mix, reviewers and reconcilers on the most-capable tier, merge/setup on the cheapest. misrankCandidates is empty — no tier looked obviously mis-assigned. A reasonable cost profile for a multi-wave feature build; the most-capable tier is concentrated on review/reconcile where judgment matters, as intended.
implication: Positive exemplar — the tier mapping and the effort-audit instrumentation worked as designed. No change indicated; keep as a calibration baseline for what a healthy mixed-tier feature run looks like.
title: Audit present and healthy but misrankCandidates empty despite mixed-tier assignment across 24 agents

evidence: At the pre-merge gate the orchestrator tried to read the last wave-merge head sha from the workflow result to assert integration-HEAD == reported-merge-sha (the checkout-drift guard). The result object did not contain that key — the parse raised a KeyError and the top-level result keys were a different summary/logs/result/progress/token shape. The orchestrator fell back to manually diffing the integration branch against base and re-running the suite itself to regain confidence, but the documented deterministic guard silently did not execute.
implication: The Step-5 guard depends on a result field the engine did not emit in this run — either a result-schema drift or a field only present on some run shapes. Make the guard tolerate the field's absence explicitly (surface 'merge-sha guard unavailable — result schema lacks waveMerges' rather than crashing) and pin the result schema the guard reads so drift is caught by a test, not at a live gate.
title: Step-5 deterministic merge-sha guard could not run: workflow result lacked the waveMerges key it keys on

evidence: The engine produces one merge commit per wave plus reconciliation merges; across this run and a prior run on the same long-lived branch these accumulated into roughly nineteen merge commits not on the base. The target repo disallowed merge-commit merges and required rebase-or-squash; the branch could not be cleanly rebased because conflict resolutions baked into the merge commits do not replay linearly. The host platform's merge button was disabled, and the operator had to be pulled in to choose squash to land the work — a real blocker at the very end of an otherwise clean run.
implication: The engine's wave-by-wave merge topology is incompatible with rebase-only or merge-commit-forbidding repos. The finishing-a-development-branch handoff should detect the target repo's allowed merge methods and warn when the integration branch's merge-commit count will block rebase, recommending squash up front. Worth documenting in the engine/finishing handoff as a known interaction.
title: Accumulated wave-merge commits made the integration branch un-rebaseable, blocking the repo's only allowed merge method

evidence: Every task came back heuristic:false (markers adopted verbatim), no marker conflicts, mode parallel, a gate task correctly compiled into run config, Interfaces Consumes/Produces present with matching Depends-on edges, and a non-sealed acceptance disposition appropriate for operator-readable internal tooling. The wave shape was a real fan-out (three independent contract/docs tasks) feeding two dependent consumers. All tasks landed clean first try, zero fix iterations.
implication: Positive exemplar of the full author-then-compile contract working end to end: marker-driven edges, contract-first decomposition, gate-into-config, and a correct acceptance disposition. Keep as the reference for what 'right' looks like; nothing to change.
title: Marked plan compiled with zero heuristics and zero marker conflicts — the intended clean path

evidence: The operator authorized execution at the planning handoff and did not intervene mid-run. At the gate the orchestrator re-ran the suite itself and diffed the branch by hand to rebuild confidence the broken sha-guard would have given automatically. Later, the orchestrator initially trusted a platform API 'mergeable' field, declared the merge box stale, and was corrected only when the operator pushed it to open the actual UI — exposing the real rebase blocker. Trust was preserved, but twice the system needed a human or a manual cross-check to catch what an automated signal had gotten wrong.
implication: Two of the run's signals (the merge-sha guard, a platform mergeability field) gave false comfort or crashed; in both cases the recovery was manual. Hardening the Step-5 guard and the finishing-handoff's merge-method detection would move this attention back off the human, which is the design's stated goal.
title: Operator trust held through execution but was earned back only by manual verification and a browser check at the merge box

evidence: Roughly 18 agents, ~1M tokens, ~24 minutes. The two cross-package / state-reset-subtle tasks were assigned the stronger tier and adversarial (two-pass) review; the three routine tasks stayed cheap and lean. Reviewers and reconcile ran on the most-capable tier. misrankCandidates empty. Because every task passed clean with zero fix iterations, the run paid for review but never for rework.
implication: Positive exemplar — adversarial review was spent exactly where the operator-declared risk was, lean elsewhere, and the absence of fix rounds shows the per-task review depth was well-targeted. No change indicated.
title: Healthy adversarial-on-risky-tasks tiering; clean run amortized review cost with zero fix rounds

evidence: This bundle has planningFound:false, planPath:null, gateReport:null, every one of ~200 audited agents tagged role 'unknown', and misrankCandidates empty — yet it carries the largest audit by far (~200 agents, ~4400 turns, ~578K output tokens). The underlying activity was an improvised read-only fan-out-and-verify Workflow plus a follow-up issue-drafting Workflow, not an ultrapowers wave run at all. Harvest swept it into the ultrapowers run set because it lives under the same workflow-transcripts footprint, and the audit then tried to assign ultrapowers roles to agents that have none.
implication: Harvest should distinguish ultrapowers engine runs (integration branch, setup/merge/review/reconcile roles, a compiled plan) from arbitrary user-authored Workflows that happen to share the transcript directory. Gate on engine fingerprints (the saved-workflow meta.name, the wave/merge role pattern, planningFound) so non-engine runs are excluded or clearly tagged, rather than padding the ledger with role:'unknown' mega-runs.
title: Harvest captured a non-ultrapowers read-only Workflow run as if it were an engine run (no plan, no roles)

evidence: Across roughly 200 agents the audit assigned every single one role 'unknown' and produced no misrank candidates. The agents are all on one tier and the turn counts form a long smooth tail, but with no role labels the cost lens cannot say whether tiering was appropriate, whether reviewers were over- or under-powered, or where effort concentrated. The audit ran and totaled tokens but produced no actionable cost finding — the most expensive run in the batch is the least legible.
implication: When role inference fails wholesale (every agent 'unknown'), the audit should declare low confidence rather than emit a totals table that reads as authoritative — and this only matters because a non-engine run was harvested at all (see the harvest finding). Tie the audit's role inference to the same engine-fingerprint gate.
title: Role attribution fully collapsed to 'unknown' on a 200-agent run, so the effort audit yields no usable signal

evidence: The captured (non-engine) Workflow fanned area reviewers across a large codebase, then handed every falsifiable finding to a separate adversarial agent that re-read the cited code and tried to refute it before it counted; a meaningful fraction of raw findings were refuted, including one the orchestrator had personally seeded wrong. A downstream Workflow then re-verified every cited location at draft time and corrected line-number and path drift before filing. The striking behavior: the swarm treated its own outputs as untrusted and built falsification in as a first-class stage, catching false positives the design never told it to look for.
implication: This is exactly the adversarial-verification posture ultrapowers bakes into its reviewer/completeness roles, here emerging spontaneously in an improvised read-only swarm. It is also a frontier signal that the read/write boundary held: a large improvised swarm did only discovery/verification and never touched the merge keys. Worth studying as a model for read-only research harnesses, and as evidence the write-side determinism guard's scope (improvised = read-only) is the right line.
title: An improvised read-only swarm scaled to ~200 verify-then-synthesize agents and self-corrected its own seed claims

evidence: Recurs across 2 runs in this project. In both ultrapowers runs in the batch, the plans concentrated edits on a small number of large files (a multi-hundred-line route/god-component in one run, a cross-package generate trio in the other). One run mitigated by extracting a sub-module before fan-out; the other declared the same file-seam shaping move explicitly. Where the seam split happened, parallel width survived; where multiple tasks still shared a file, edges serialized them. The independent-file work (new hooks, new contract files, docs) parallelized cleanly in both.
implication: The project's natural code shape (large concern-dense files) is the dominant limiter on achievable wave width. ultraplan's seam-split / contract-first guidance is the correct response and demonstrably works when applied; reinforce that the wave-plan render should make same-file contention visible so operators see the serialization cost of not splitting before they launch.
title: Recurring shape across the project: many implementation tasks converging on one or two large shared files
